name: Other bacterial intestinal infections
chapter: Chapter I Infectious and parasitic diseases
description: Bacterial infections affecting the intestinal tract.

name: Viral and other specified intestinal infections
chapter: Chapter I Infectious and parasitic diseases
description: influenza with involvement of gastrointestinal tract

name: Other gastroenteritis and colitis of infectious and unspecified origin
chapter: Chapter I Infectious and parasitic diseases
description: An infectious illness affecting the stomach or intestines, causing symptoms such as diarrhoea and inflammation, where the exact cause is unknown or not specified.

name: Other sepsis
chapter: Chapter I Infectious and parasitic diseases
description: A severe bloodstream infection caused by bacteria that affect the whole body.

name: Other bacterial diseases
chapter: Chapter I Infectious and parasitic diseases

name: Chronic viral hepatitis C
chapter: Chapter I Infectious and parasitic diseases

name: viral infection of unspecified site
chapter: Chapter I Infectious and parasitic diseases
description: General or unnamed viral illnesses (such as an unspecified systemic viral syndrome) where the virus affects the body but the exact organ system or localized site is not identified

name: Candidiasis
chapter: Chapter I Infectious and parasitic diseases
description: Fungal or yeast infection caused by species of the genus Candida (most commonly Candida albicans). It includes conditions like oral thrush, cutaneous diaper rashes, or vaginal yeast infections.
inclusions: candidosis | moniliasis

name: Streptococcus and staphylococcus as the cause of diseases classified to other chapter
chapter: Chapter I Infectious and parasitic diseases
description: This code is never used alone as a primary diagnosis. It is a supplementary "organism" code added to a primary condition (like a urinary tract infection or a wound infection) to explicitly identify that a Strep, Staph, or Enterococcus bacterium caused it

name: Other specified bacterial agents as the cause of diseases classified to other chapters
chapter: Chapter I Infectious and parasitic diseases
description: Similar to B95, this is a non-primary secondary code used to log the specific bacterial cause of a localized infection. It covers subcategories for bacteria like E. coli, Klebsiella pneumoniae, or Pseudomonas.

name: Malignant neoplasm of base of tongue
description: Primary, invasive malignant tumor located specifically at the very back (posterior third) of the tongue, where it connects to the throat.
inclusions: Dorsal surface of base of tongue | Fixed part of tongue NOS | Posterior third of tongue

name: Malignant neoplasm of other and unspecified parts of tongue
description:
Dorsal surface of tongue
Anterior two-thirds of tongue, dorsal surface

name: Malignant neoplasm of border of tongue
description: Cancer originating in the sides (lateral margins) or tip of the tongue
inclusions: Tip of tongue

name: Malignant neoplasm of tongue, unspecified. [1]
description: Tongue cancer where the specific sub-site or margin is not documented in the medical records

name: Malignant neoplasm of floor of mouth
description: Cancer developing in the mucosal tissue located on the lower surface of the mouth beneath the tongue

name: Malignant neoplasm of parotid gland
description: Cancer in the largest pair of salivary glands located just in front of the ears. The "X" acts as a structural placeholder for standard formatting

name: Malignant neoplasm of tonsil
description: Cancer originating within the tonsillar tissue of the throat

name: Malignant neoplasm of tonsil, unspecified.
description: Tonsillar cancer where the specific area (such as the tonsillar pillar or fossa) is not detailed.
inclusions: Tonsil: NOS | Tonsil: faucial | Tonsil: palatine

name: Malignant neoplasm of oropharynx.
description: Cancer involving the middle section of the throat behind the mouth, which includes the back of the tongue and soft palate

name: Malignant neoplasm of oropharynx, unspecified
description: Oropharyngeal cancer where the exact local site cannot be isolated or is not specified

name: Malignant neoplasm of pyriform sinus
description: Cancer located in the recess or hidden structural pouch on either side of the larynx (voice box entrance).
inclusions: Piriform fossa

name: Malignant neoplasm of the esophagus
description: The overarching baseline code for primary cancer of the muscular tube connecting the throat to the stomach

name: Malignant neoplasm of upper third of esophagus
description: Cancer located specifically in the cervical/upper section of the esophagus closest to the throat.

name: Malignant neoplasm of the middle third of the esophagus
description: Cancer located in the thoracic or middle section of the esophagus behind the chest cavity.

name: Malignant neoplasm of lower third of esophagus
description: Cancer located in the abdominal or lower section of the esophagus just before it meets the stomach junction.

name: Malignant neoplasm of esophagus, unspecified
description: cancer of the gullet, where the specific location in the esophagus is not stated or known

name: Malignant neoplasm of the stomach
description: cancerous growth that originates in the tissues and inner lining of the stomach

name: Cardia (gastro-oesophageal junction)
description: the primary cancerous tumor located at the very top section of the stomach, where it connects to the esophagus
inclusions: Cardiac orifice | Cardio-oesophageal junction | Gastro-oesophageal junction | Oesophagus and stomach

name: Stomach, unspecified [1]
description: the primary stomach cancer ( gastric cancer) when the physician's documentation does not specify exactly which part of the stomach the tumor is located in, or if the exact origin site connot be determined.
inclusions: Gastric cancer NOS

name: Stomach, unspecified [1]
description: the primary stomach cancer ( gastric cancer) when the physician's documentation does not specify exactly which part of the stomach the tumor is located in, or if the exact origin site connot be determined.

name: Malignant neoplasm of small intestine.
description: Cancerous growth that originates in the tissues of the small bowel

name: Malignant neoplasm of duodenum.
description: Cancerous tumor located in the duodenum, which is the first, shortest, and widest part of the small intestine that connects directly to the exit valve of the stomach

name: Malignant neoplasm of ileum
description: Cancerous tumor located in he ileum, which is the third, final, and longest section of the small intestine. It connects the middle section of the small bowel (the jejunum) to the large intestine(the colon)

name: Malignant neoplasm of colon.
description: Cancerous growth that originates in the tissues and inner lining of the large bowel

name: Malignant neoplasm of cecum
description: The primary cancerous tumor located in the cecum, which is the large, blind-ended pouch that forms the very beginning of the large intestine(colon)
inclusions: Ileocaecal valve

name: Malignant neoplasm of the Appendix
description: The primary cancerous tumor located within the appendix( appendix vermiformis), which is the small, finger-like pouch projecting out from the cecum in the lower right side of the abdomen.

name: Ascending colon (the upward section on the right side of the abdomen)
description: The primary cancerous tumor located in the first long section of the large intestine.

name: Hepatic flexure (the bend near the liver)
description: The primary cancer located at the sharp bend or turn of the colon on the upper right side of the abdomen, right beneath the liver( hepar). It marks the transition point where the upward-running ascending colon turns to cross the body horizontally.

name: Transverse colon (the section crossing from right to left)
description: The primary cancer located in the long, horizontal segment of the large intestine that crosses the upper abdomen from right to left, sitting between the hepatic and splenic flexures.

name: Splenic flexure (the bend near the spleen)
description: The primary cancer located at the sharp bend of the colon in the upper left side of the abdomen, right next to the spleen. It marks the transition point where the horizontal transverse colon turns downward

name: Descending colon (the downward section on the left side)
description: The  This refers to a primary cancer located in the downward-running segment of the large intestine on the left side of the abdomen, moving waste down from the splenic flexure toward the pelvis.

name: Sigmoid colon (the S-shaped loop connecting to the rectum)
description: The primary cancer located in the S-shaped loop of the colon in the lower-left pelvis. This segment is responsible for storing waste before it passes into the rectum.
inclusions: Sigmoid (flexure)

name: Colon, unspecified
description: This is a general or "Not Otherwise Specified" (NOS) code used when clinical records or pathology reports diagnose a primary colon cancer but fail to specify exactly which anatomical segment (such as ascending, transverse, or sigmoid) the tumor originated in.
inclusions: Large intestine NOS

name: Malignant neoplasm of rectosigmoid junction (border between the colon and rectum)
description: Cancer located right on the anatomical border where the S-shaped lower colon turns into the rectum.
inclusions: Colon with rectum | Rectosigmoid (colon)

name: Malignant neoplasm of rectosigmoid junction (border between the colon and rectum)
description: Cancer located right on the anatomical border where the S-shaped lower colon turns into the rectum.
inclusions: Colon with rectum | Rectosigmoid (colon)

name: Malignant neoplasm of rectum.
description: Cancer originating in the final 12â€“15 centimeters of the large intestine, ending just above the anal canal.
inclusions: Rectal ampulla

name: Malignant neoplasm of rectum.
description: Cancer originating in the final 12â€“15 centimeters of the large intestine, ending just above the anal canal.
inclusions: Rectal ampulla

name: Malignant neoplasm of anus and anal canal.
description: Cancer starting in the very end of the digestive tract, where waste exits the body.

name: Malignant neoplasm of anus, unspecified.
description: Cancer starting in the very end of the digestive tract, where waste exits the body.

name: Malignant neoplasm of liver and intrahepatic bile ducts.
description: Primary cancer of the liver tissue when the specific cell type isn't documented.

name: Liver cell carcinoma (Hepatocellular carcinoma / hepatoma).
description: Specifically Hepatocellular Carcinoma (HCC), which starts in the main liver cells (hepatocytes). It is the most common form of primary liver cancer.
inclusions: Hepatocellular carcinoma | Hepatoma

name: Intrahepatic bile duct carcinoma (Cholangiocarcinoma).
description: Also known as cholangiocarcinoma, this cancer starts in the tiny fluid-drainage tubes located inside the liver.
inclusions: Cholangiocarcinoma

name: Malignant neoplasm of liver, unspecified.
description: Primary cancer of the liver tissue when the specific cell type isn't documented.

name: Malignant neoplasm of gallbladder.
description: Cancer of the small, pear-shaped organ beneath the liver that stores bile.

name: Malignant neoplasm of gallbladder.
description: Cancer of the small, pear-shaped organ beneath the liver that stores bile.

name: Malignant neoplasm of other and unspecified parts of biliary tract (such as the extrahepatic bile duct).
description: Cancer in the fluid-drainage system outside the liver, including the common bile duct.

name: Malignant neoplasm of pancreas.
description: Cancer of the hormone and enzyme-producing gland behind the stomach.

name: Malignant neoplasm of head of pancreas.
description: These codes pinpoint the exact section of the pancreas. The "head" is next to the small intestine, the "body" is the middle, and the "tail" points toward the spleen.

name: Malignant neoplasm of body of pancreas.
description: These codes pinpoint the exact section of the pancreas. The "head" is next to the small intestine, the "body" is the middle, and the "tail" points toward the spleen.

name: Malignant neoplasm of tail of pancreas.
description: These codes pinpoint the exact section of the pancreas. The "head" is next to the small intestine, the "body" is the middle, and the "tail" points toward the spleen.

name: Malignant neoplasm of pancreas, unspecified.
description: Cancer of the hormone and enzyme-producing gland behind the stomach.

name: Malignant neoplasm of other and ill-defined digestive organs.
description: A fallback code used when a tumor spans multiple digestive organs and its exact starting point cannot be isolated.

name: Malignant neoplasm of larynx (voice box).
description: Cancer of the voice box. "Supraglottis" specifically means it is located in the upper section above the vocal cords.

name: Malignant neoplasm of supraglottis (upper portion of larynx)
description: Cancer of the voice box. "Supraglottis" specifically means it is located in the upper section above the vocal cords.
inclusions: Aryepiglottic fold, laryngeal aspect | Epiglottis (suprahyoid portion) NOS | Extrinsic larynx | False vocal cord | Posterior (laryngeal) surface of epiglottis | Ventricular bands

name: Malignant neoplasm of bronchus and lung.
description: General lung cancer where the specific lobe or airway is not documented.

name: Malignant neoplasm of main bronchus
description: Cancer located in the large, primary airways leading directly from the windpipe into the lungs.
inclusions: Carina | Hilus (of lung)

name: Malignant neoplasm of upper lobe, bronchus or lung.
description: These specify the exact anatomical section of the lung where the tumor is growing.

name: Malignant neoplasm of middle lobe, bronchus or lung.
description: These specify the exact anatomical section of the lung where the tumor is growing.

name: Malignant neoplasm of lower lobe, bronchus or lung.
description: These specify the exact anatomical section of the lung where the tumor is growing.

name: Malignant neoplasm of  bronchus or lung, unspecified.
description: General lung cancer where the specific lobe or airway is not documented.

name: Malignant neoplasm of  bronchus or lung, unspecified.
description: General lung cancer where the specific lobe or airway is not documented.

name: Malignant neoplasm of heart, mediastinum, and pleura
description: Rare tumors of the heart, the space between the lungs (mediastinum), or the chest cavity lining.

name: Malignant neoplasm of mandible (lower jaw bone).
description: A primary bone cancer originating inside the lower jawbone.
inclusions: Lower jaw bone

name: Malignant melanoma of skin
description: An aggressive type of skin cancer that begins in the pigment-producing cells (melanocytes). The sub-codes track whether it is located on the face, the trunk (chest/back), or the lower limbs (legs/hip).
inclusions: morphology codes M872-M879 with behaviour code /3

name: Malignant melanoma of other and unspecified parts of face.
description: An aggressive type of skin cancer that begins in the pigment-producing cells (melanocytes). The sub-codes track whether it is located on the face, the trunk (chest/back), or the lower limbs (legs/hip).

name: Malignant melanoma of trunk.
description: An aggressive type of skin cancer that begins in the pigment-producing cells (melanocytes). The sub-codes track whether it is located on the face, the trunk (chest/back), or the lower limbs (legs/hip).
inclusions: Anal: margin | Anal: skin | Perianal skin | Skin of breast

name: Malignant melanoma of lower limb, including hip.
description: An aggressive type of skin cancer that begins in the pigment-producing cells (melanocytes). The sub-codes track whether it is located on the face, the trunk (chest/back), or the lower limbs (legs/hip).

name: Other malignant neoplasms of skin (covers non-melanoma skin cancers like basal cell or squamous cell carcinoma).
description: Non-melanoma skin cancers, most commonly Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) or Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC).
inclusions: malignant neoplasm of: sebaceous glands | malignant neoplasm of: sweat glands

name: Mesothelioma.
description: A rare cancer highly associated with asbestos exposure, usually affecting the lining of the lungs or abdomen.
inclusions: morphology code M905 with behaviour code /3

name: Mesothelioma,unspecified.
description: A rare cancer highly associated with asbestos exposure, usually affecting the lining of the lungs or abdomen.

name: Malignant neoplasm of retroperitoneum and peritoneum (lining of the abdominal cavity).
description: Cancer of the specialized membranes that line the inside of the abdominal cavity and wrap around the abdominal organs.

name: Malignant neoplasm of other connective and soft tissue
description: Also known as sarcomas, these are cancers that start in muscles, fat, tendons, or deep skin tissues.
inclusions: blood vessel | bursa | cartilage | fascia | fat | ligament, except uterine | lymphatic vessel | muscle | synovia | tendon (sheath)

name: Malignant neoplasm of breast.
description: Breast cancer where the specific quadrant of the tissue is not detailed.
inclusions: connective tissue of breast

name: Malignant neoplasm of nipple and areola.
description: Cancer located directly in the nipple or the dark ring of skin surrounding it.

name: Malignant neoplasm of upper-inner quadrant of breast
description: Doctors divide the breast into four quadrants like a map; these codes track whether the tumor is on the upper side closer to the breastbone (inner) or closer to the armpit (outer).

name: Malignant neoplasm of upper-outer quadrant of breast.
description: Doctors divide the breast into four quadrants like a map; these codes track whether the tumor is on the upper side closer to the breastbone (inner) or closer to the armpit (outer).

name: Malignant neoplasm of overlapping sites of breast.
description: Used when a single breast tumor is large enough to bridge across multiple quadrants.

name: Malignant neoplasm of breast, unspecified.
description: Breast cancer where the specific quadrant of the tissue is not detailed.

name: Malignant neoplasm of vulva.
description: Cancer of the external female genitalia.

name: Malignant neoplasm of vulva, unspecified.
description: Cancer of the external female genitalia.
inclusions: External female genitalia NOS | Pudendum

name: Malignant neoplasm of cervix uteri.
description: Cancer of the cervix, which is the narrow neck of the uterus connecting to the vagina.

name: Malignant neoplasm of cervix uteri, unspecified.
description: Cancer of the cervix, which is the narrow neck of the uterus connecting to the vagina.

name: Malignant neoplasm of corpus uteri (body of the uterus).
description: Cancer of the main body of the uterus. "Endometrium" specifically means it started in the inner tissue lining that sheds during a menstrual cycle.

name: Malignant neoplasm of endometrium (inner lining of the uterus).
description: Cancer of the main body of the uterus. "Endometrium" specifically means it started in the inner tissue lining that sheds during a menstrual cycle.

name: Malignant neoplasm of uterus, part unspecified
description: Uterine cancer where the record does not clarify if it started in the cervix or the inner body.

name: Malignant neoplasm of uterus, part unspecified
description: Uterine cancer where the record does not clarify if it started in the cervix or the inner body.

name: Malignant neoplasm of ovary.
description: Cancer originating in the female egg-producing reproductive glands.

name: Malignant neoplasm of ovary.
description: Cancer originating in the female egg-producing reproductive glands.

name: Malignant neoplasm of prostate.
description: Cancer of the walnut-sized gland in men that helps produce seminal fluid

name: Malignant neoplasm of prostate.
description: Cancer of the walnut-sized gland in men that helps produce seminal fluid

name: Malignant neoplasm of prostate.
description: Cancer of the walnut-sized gland in men that helps produce seminal fluid

name: Malignant neoplasm of testis.
description: Cancer of the male reproductive glands located inside the scrotum.

name: Malignant neoplasm of testis, unspecified.
description: Cancer of the male reproductive glands located inside the scrotum.

name: Malignant neoplasm of kidney, except renal pelvis.
description: Cancer originating in the main body of the kidney where blood is filtered.

name: Malignant neoplasm of kidney, except renal pelvis.
description: Cancer originating in the main body of the kidney where blood is filtered.

name: Malignant neoplasm of renal pelvis.
description: Cancer located specifically in the funnel-like basin at the center of the kidney where urine collects before flowing down to the bladder.
inclusions: Pelviureteric junction | Renal calyces

name: Malignant neoplasm of renal pelvis.
description: Cancer located specifically in the funnel-like basin at the center of the kidney where urine collects before flowing down to the bladder.
inclusions: Pelviureteric junction | Renal calyces

name: Malignant neoplasm of bladder
description: Cancer originating in the tissues of the urinary bladder.

name: Malignant neoplasm of bladder, unspecified
description: Bladder cancer where the specific anatomical wall (e.g., dome, lateral wall, bladder neck) is not specified in the records.

name: Malignant neoplasm of choroid
description: A primary cancer of the eye, specifically originating in the choroidâ€”the vascular layer of tissue filled with blood vessels that sits between the retina and the sclera (white of the eye).

name: Malignant neoplasm of brain
description: Primary cancer originating directly in the tissues of the brain.

name: Malignant neoplasm of brain, unspecified
description: Brain cancer where the specific lobe (frontal, temporal, occipital, etc.) or brainstem area is not documented.

name: Malignant neoplasm of thyroid gland
description: Cancer originating in the butterfly-shaped thyroid gland at the base of the neck. The "X" functions as a standard software data structural placeholder.

name: Malignant neoplasm of thyroid gland
description: Cancer originating in the butterfly-shaped thyroid gland at the base of the neck. The "X" functions as a standard software data structural placeholder.

name: Malignant neoplasm of other and ill-defined sites
description: Fallback category for cancers where the primary site is known to be in a general region but cannot be isolated to a single organ.

name: Malignant neoplasm of abdomen
description: A primary tumor located within the abdominal cavity walls or general abdominal region that cannot be assigned to a more specific organ (like the liver or bowel).

name: Secondary and unspecified malignant neoplasm of lymph nodes
description: The baseline parent code for cancer that has metastasized to the lymph nodes.

name: Secondary and unspecified malignant neoplasm of lymph nodes of head, face and neck
description: Cancer that has spread to the lymph nodes in the neck and head area (such as cervical or submandibular nodes).
inclusions: Supraclavicular lymph nodes

name: Secondary and unspecified malignant neoplasm of intrathoracic lymph nodes
description: Cancer that has spread to the lymph nodes inside the chest cavity (such as mediastinal or hilar nodes).

name: Secondary and unspecified malignant neoplasm of intra-abdominal lymph nodes
description: Cancer that has spread to the lymph nodes located inside the abdomen (such as mesenteric or para-aortic nodes).

name: Secondary and unspecified malignant neoplasm of axillary and upper limb lymph nodes
description: Cancer that has spread to the lymph nodes in the armpit (axilla) or arm (frequently tracked during breast cancer treatments).
inclusions: Pectoral lymph nodes

name: Secondary and unspecified malignant neoplasm of inguinal and lower limb lymph nodes
description: Cancer that has spread to the lymph nodes in the groin (inguinal area) or leg.

name: Secondary and unspecified malignant neoplasm of intrapelvic lymph nodes
description: Cancer that has spread to the lymph nodes located within the lower pelvis (such as iliac nodes)

name: Secondary and unspecified malignant neoplasm of lymph node, unspecified
description: Cancer found in a lymph node, but the medical record does not state where that lymph node is located in the body.

name: Secondary malignant neoplasm of respiratory and digestive organs
description: The category parent code for cancer spreading to chest or gut organs.

name: Secondary malignant neoplasm of lung
description: Cancer that started elsewhere (e.g., breast, colon) and has spread to form tumors in the lungs.

name: Secondary malignant neoplasm of mediastinum
description: Metastatic tumors in the central area of the chest cavity between the lungs.

name: Secondary malignant neoplasm of pleura
description: Cancer that has spread to the outer protective lining surrounding the lungs
inclusions: Malignant pleural effusion NOS

name: Secondary malignant neoplasm of pleura
description: Cancer that has spread to the outer protective lining surrounding the lungs

name: Secondary malignant neoplasm of large intestine and rectum
description: Metastatic tumors that have nested inside the large bowel from a different primary cancer site.

name: Secondary malignant neoplasm of retroperitoneum and peritoneum
description: Cancer that has spread across the membranes lining the inner walls of the abdomen.
inclusions: Malignant ascites NOS

name: Secondary malignant neoplasm of liver and intrahepatic bile duct
description: Cancer that has metastasized into the liver tissue from another organ (extremely common with primary digestive cancers).

name: Secondary malignant neoplasm of other and unspecified digestive organs
description: Cancer spreading to miscellaneous digestive structures like the stomach or pancreas

name: Secondary malignant neoplasm of other and unspecified sites
description: Category parent code for advanced metastases to other areas of the body.

name: Secondary malignant neoplasm of kidney and renal pelvis
description: Cancer that has spread into the tissue of the kidneys from a different primary site.

name: Secondary malignant neoplasm of bladder and other and unspecified urinary organs
description: Metastatic cancer nesting inside the urinary tract structures.

name: Secondary malignant neoplasm of brain and cerebral meninges
description: Cancer that started elsewhere in the body and has metastasized to the brain tissues or its surrounding membranes.

name: Secondary malignant neoplasm of bone and bone marrow
description: Metastatic cancer that has spread into the skeletal system or the blood-producing marrow inside the bones.

name: Secondary malignant neoplasm of bone and bone marrow
description: Metastatic cancer that has spread into the skeletal system or the blood-producing marrow inside the bones.

name: Secondary malignant neoplasm of ovary
description: Metastatic cancer that has spread to the ovaries (often from primary stomach or colon cancers, historically referred to as a Krukenberg tumor).

name: Secondary malignant neoplasm of adrenal gland
description: Cancer that has spread to the small hormone-producing glands sitting on top of the kidneys.

name: Secondary malignant neoplasm of other specified sites
description: Metastasis to specific sites not covered in other sections, such as the skin or muscles.

name: Secondary malignant neoplasm of unspecified site
description: Advanced metastatic disease where the secondary tumors are noted, but their exact physical sites are not listed in the data.
inclusions: Carcinomatosis (secondary) | Disseminated (secondary): cancer NOS | Disseminated (secondary): malignancy NOS | Generalized (secondary): cancer NOS | Generalized (secondary): malignancy NOS | Multiple secondary cancer NOS | Sarcomatosis (secondary) NOS

name: Malignant neoplasm without specification of site
description: Commonly referred to in medicine as Cancer of Unknown Primary (CUP). This code is used when a patient has a verified primary malignant cancer, but doctors are entirely unable to determine what organ or tissue it originally started in.

name: Malignant neoplasm without specification of site
description: Commonly referred to in medicine as Cancer of Unknown Primary (CUP). This code is used when a patient has a verified primary malignant cancer, but doctors are entirely unable to determine what organ or tissue it originally started in.

name: Hodgkin lymphoma : Blood cancer
description: A specific type of lymphoma characterized by the presence of unique, abnormal cells called Reed-Sternberg cells.

name: Follicular lymphoma : Blood cancer
description: A slow-growing (indolent) form of Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma that develops in circular patterns inside the lymph nodes.
inclusions: follicular lymphoma with or without diffuse areas

name: Follicular lymphoma Grade II : Blood cancer
description: Follicular lymphoma showing a moderate mix of small and large abnormal cells under a microscope.

name: Follicular lymphoma, unspecified : Blood cancer
description: Follicular lymphoma where the specific grade (I, II, or III) is not documented.
inclusions: Nodular lymphoma NOS

name: Non-follicular lymphoma
description: A broad grouping of Non-Hodgkin Lymphomas that grow more diffusely through the lymph node tissue rather than in tight circular clusters (includes aggressive types like Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma).

name: Other and unspecified types of non-Hodgkin lymphoma
description: A general category code used when a patient is diagnosed with Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma, but the specific cellular subtype is not fully classified or specified in the report.

name: Multiple myeloma and malignant plasma cell neoplasms
description: A primary blood cancer originating in the bone marrow's plasma cells.

name: Multiple myeloma
description: The specific code for classic systemic multiple myeloma.
inclusions: Kahler disease | Medullary plasmacytoma | Myelomatosis | Plasma cell myeloma

name: Lymphoid Leukemia
description: The bone marrow produces too many abnormal lymphocytes, which crowd out healthy blood cells. [1]

name: Myeloid Leukemia
description: The bone marrow produces abnormal, immature myeloid cells (blasts) that cannot function properly.
inclusions: leukaemia: granulocytic | leukaemia: myelogenous

name: Carcinoma in situ of middle ear and respiratory system
description: Pre-cancerous cells confined strictly to the surface lining of the airways, lungs, or ear cavity.

name: Melanoma in situ
description: Stage 0 melanoma where abnormal pigment cells are completely restricted to the outermost layer of skin (epidermis).
inclusions: morphology codes M872-M879 with behaviour code /2

name: Carcinoma in situ of skin
description: Non-melanoma pre-cancerous skin lesions (such as Bowenâ€™s disease) that have not invaded deeper tissues.

name: Carcinoma in situ of breast
description: Pre-cancerous cells confined inside the milk ducts (DCIS) or lobules (LCIS) of the breast.

name: Carcinoma in situ of cervix uteri
description: Severe cervical dysplasia or Stage 0 cancer confined to the surface of the cervix.
inclusions: cervical intraepithelial neoplasia [CIN], grade III, with or without mention of severe dysplasia

name: Carcinoma in situ of other and unspecified genital organs
description: Stage 0 surface cell abnormalities of the reproductive tract.

name: Carcinoma in situ of other and unspecified sites
description: Fallback code for pre-cancerous surface lesions at unspecified anatomical sites.

name: Benign neoplasm of colon, rectum, anus and anal canal
description: Non-cancerous growths, most commonly common colon polyps.

name: Benign neoplasm of other and ill-defined parts of digestive system
description: Non-cancerous tumors inside the esophagus, stomach, or small bowel.

name: Melanocytic nevi
description: The medical classification code for standard, non-cancerous skin moles.
inclusions: morphology codes M872-M879 with behaviour code /0 | naevus: NOS | naevus: blue | naevus: hairy | naevus: pigmented

name: Other benign neoplasms of skin
description: Non-cancerous non-mole skin growths (such as benign skin cysts or dermatofibromas).
inclusions: benign neoplasm of: hair follicles | benign neoplasm of: sebaceous glands | benign neoplasm of: sweat glands

name: Benign neoplasm of breast
description: Non-cancerous breast lumps, such as a fibroadenoma.
inclusions: Breast: connective tissue | Breast: soft parts

name: Leiomyoma of uterus
description: Commonly known as uterine fibroids, which are benign smooth-muscle tumors in the uterine wall.
inclusions: benign neoplasms of uterus with morphology code M889 and behaviour code /0 | fibromyoma of uterus

name: Benign neoplasm of ovary
description: Non-cancerous growths or benign cysts originating in the ovary.

name: Benign neoplasm of urinary organs
description: Non-cancerous tumors located inside the kidneys or bladder.

name: Benign neoplasm of meninges
description: A non-cancerous tumor growing in the membranes (meninges) that protect the brain and spinal cord.

name: Benign neoplasm of other and unspecified endocrine glands
description: Non-cancerous tumors of glands like the adrenal or pituitary glands.

name: Neoplasms of uncertain or unknown behavior
description: A cancerous growth that pathology reports cannot determine if a tumor is completely begin or fully malignant.

name: Neoplasms of uncertain or unknown behavior
description: A cancerous growth that pathology reports cannot determine if a tumor is completely begin or fully malignant.

name: Neoplasms of uncertain or unknown behavior
description: A cancerous growth that pathology reports cannot determine if a tumor is completely begin or fully malignant.

name: Neoplasms of uncertain or unknown behavior
description: A cancerous growth that pathology reports cannot determine if a tumor is completely begin or fully malignant.

name: Neoplasms of uncertain or unknown behavior
description: A cancerous growth that pathology reports cannot determine if a tumor is completely begin or fully malignant.
inclusions: Alkylating-agent-related myelodysplastic syndrome | Epipodophyllotoxin-related myelodysplastic syndrome | Therapy-related myelodysplastic syndrome NOS

name: Neoplasms of uncertain or unknown behavior
description: A cancerous growth that pathology reports cannot determine if a tumor is completely begin or fully malignant.

name: Iron deficiency anemia
chapter: Chapter III: Diseases of the Blood and Blood-forming Organs
description: Low red blood cell counts or hemoglobin caused by a lack of iron in the body.
inclusions: anaemia: asiderotic | anaemia: hypochromic

name: Vitamin B12 deficiency anemia
chapter: Chapter III: Diseases of the Blood and Blood-forming Organs
description: Anemia caused by a lack of dietary B12 or an inability to absorb it (pernicious anemia).

The duration is variable and the degree of severity ranges from mild to very severe.
inclusions: acute or subacute: brain syndrome | acute or subacute: confusional state (nonalcoholic) | acute or subacute: infective psychosis | acute or subacute: organic reaction | acute or subacute: psycho-organic syndrome

name: Other mental disorders due to brain damage and dysfunction and to physical disease

name: Mental and behavioural disorders due to use of alcohol
chapter: Chapter IIIII: Mental and behavioural disorders due to psychoactive substance use

name: Mental and behavioural disorders due to use of tobacco
chapter: Chapter IIIII: Mental and behavioural disorders due to psychoactive substance use

name: Schizophrenia
chapter: Chapter IIIII: Mental and Behavioral Disorders
description: The schizophrenic disorders are characterized in general by fundamental and characteristic distortions of thinking and perception, and affects that are inappropriate or blunted. Clear consciousness and intellectual capacity are usually maintained although certain cognitive deficits may evolve in the course of time. The most important psychopathological phenomena include thought echo; thought insertion or withdrawal; thought broadcasting; delusional perception and delusions of control; influence or passivity; hallucinatory voices commenting or discussing the patient in the third person; thought disorders and negative symptoms.

name: Delusional disorder
chapter: Chapter IIIII: Mental and Behavioral Disorders
description: A disorder characterized by the development either of a single delusion or of a set of related delusions that are usually persistent and sometimes lifelong. The content of the delusion or delusions is very variable. Clear and persistent auditory hallucinations (voices), schizophrenic symptoms such as delusions of control and marked blunting of affect, and definite evidence of brain disease are all incompatible with this diagnosis. However, the presence of occasional or transitory auditory hallucinations, particularly in elderly patients, does not rule out this diagnosis, provided that they are not typically schizophrenic and form only a small part of the overall clinical picture.

name: Acute and transient psychotic disorders
chapter: Chapter IIIII: Mental and Behavioral Disorders
description: A heterogeneous group of disorders characterized by the acute onset of psychotic symptoms such as delusions, hallucinations, and perceptual disturbances, and by the severe disruption of ordinary behaviour. Acute onset is defined as a crescendo development of a clearly abnormal clinical picture in about two weeks or less. For these disorders there is no evidence of organic causation. Perplexity and puzzlement are often present but disorientation for time, place and person is not persistent or severe enough to justify a diagnosis of organically caused delirium (F05.-). Complete recovery usually occurs within a few months, often within a few weeks or even days. If the disorder persists, a change in classification will be necessary. The disorder may or may not be associated with acute stress, defined as usually stressful events preceding the onset by one to two weeks.

name: Schizoaffective disorders
chapter: Chapter IIIII: Mental and Behavioral Disorders
description: Episodic disorders in which both affective and schizophrenic symptoms are prominent but which do not justify a diagnosis of either schizophrenia or depressive or manic episodes. Other conditions in which affective symptoms are superimposed on a pre-existing schizophrenic illness, or co-exist or alternate with persistent delusional disorders of other kinds, are classified under F20-F29. Mood-incongruent psychotic symptoms in affective disorders do not justify a diagnosis of schizoaffective disorder.

name: Unspecified nonorganic psychosis
chapter: Chapter IIIII: Mental and Behavioral Disorders
inclusions: Psychosis NOS

name: Manic episode
chapter: Chapter IIIII: Mental and Behavioral Disorders
description: All the subdivisions of this category should be used only for a single episode. Hypomanic or manic episodes in individuals who have had one or more previous affective episodes (depressive, hypomanic, manic, or mixed) should be coded as bipolar affective disorder
inclusions: bipolar disorder, single manic episode

name: Bipolar affective disorder
chapter: Chapter IIIII: Mental and Behavioral Disorders
description: A disorder characterized by two or more episodes in which the patient's mood and activity levels are significantly disturbed, this disturbance consisting on some occasions of an elevation of mood and increased energy and activity (hypomania or mania) and on others of a lowering of mood and decreased energy and activity (depression). Repeated episodes of hypomania or mania only are classified as bipolar.
inclusions: manic depression | manic-depressive: illness | manic-depressive: psychosis | manic-depressive: reaction

name: Depressive episode
chapter: Chapter IIIII: Mental and Behavioral Disorders
description: In typical mild, moderate, or severe depressive episodes, the patient suffers from lowering of mood, reduction of energy, and decrease in activity. Capacity for enjoyment, interest, and concentration is reduced, and marked tiredness after even minimum effort is common. Sleep is usually disturbed and appetite diminished. Self-esteem and self-confidence are almost always reduced and, even in the mild form, some ideas of guilt or worthlessness are often present. The lowered mood varies little from day to day, is unresponsive to circumstances and may be accompanied by so-called "somatic" symptoms, such as loss of interest and pleasurable feelings, waking in the morning several hours before the usual time, depression worst in the morning, marked psychomotor retardation, agitation, loss of appetite, weight loss, and loss of libido. Depending upon the number and severity of the symptoms, a depressive episode may be specified as mild, moderate or severe.
inclusions: single episodes of: depressive reaction | single episodes of: psychogenic depression | single episodes of: reactive depression

name: Recurrent depressive disorder
chapter: Chapter IIIII: Mental and Behavioral Disorders
description: A disorder characterized by repeated episodes of depression as described for depressive episode (F32.-), without any history of independent episodes of mood elevation and increased energy (mania). There may, however, be brief episodes of mild mood elevation and overactivity (hypomania) immediately after a depressive episode, sometimes precipitated by antidepressant treatment. The more severe forms of recurrent depressive disorder (F33.2 and F33.3) have much in common with earlier concepts such as manic-depressive depression, melancholia, vital depression and endogenous depression. The first episode may occur at any age from childhood to old age, the onset may be either acute or insidious, and the duration varies from a few weeks to many months. The risk that a patient with recurrent depressive disorder will have an episode of mania never disappears completely, however many depressive episodes have been experienced. If such an episode does occur, the diagnosis should be changed to bipolar affective disorder (F31.-).
inclusions: recurrent episodes of: depressive reaction | recurrent episodes of: psychogenic depression | recurrent episodes of: reactive depression | seasonal depressive disorder

name: Unspecified mood [affective] disorder
chapter: Chapter IIIII: Mental and Behavioral Disorders
inclusions: Affective psychosis NOS

name: Other anxiety disorders
chapter: Chapter IIIII: Mental and Behavioral Disorders
description: Disorders in which manifestation of anxiety is the major symptom and is not restricted to any particular environmental situation. Depressive and obsessional symptoms, and even some elements of phobic anxiety, may also be present, provided that they are clearly secondary or less severe.

name: Specific developmental disorders of scholastic skills
chapter: Chapter IIIII: Mental and Behavioral Disorders
description: Disorders in which the normal patterns of skill acquisition are disturbed from the early stages of development. This is not simply a consequence of a lack of opportunity to learn, it is not solely a result of mental retardation, and it is not due to any form of acquired brain trauma or disease

name: Parkinson disease
chapter: Chapter : VI Diseases of the nervous system
description: A progressive nervous system disorder that primarily affects movement. It occurs when nerve cells in a specific part of the brain (the substantia nigra) die off. These cells normally produce a chemical messenger called dopamine.   Without enough dopamine, the brain cannot smoothly send signals to the muscles. This leads to the classic physical signs of the disease: rhythmic shaking (resting tremors, often starting in one hand), severe muscle stiffness, and extremely slowed movement (bradykinesia). As it advances, it alters gait, causing a stooped posture and a shuffling walk.
inclusions: Hemiparkinsonism | Paralysis agitans | Parkinsonism or Parkinson disease: NOS | Parkinsonism or Parkinson disease: idiopathic | Parkinsonism or Parkinson disease: primary

name: Other extrapyramidal and movement disorders
chapter: Chapter : VI Diseases of the nervous system
description: This is a "catch-all" category for involuntary movement issues that look like Parkinson's but are caused by different brain anomalies or medications.    The "extrapyramidal system" is the brain's network for regulating involuntary movements (like swinging your arms while walking). When compromised, patients experience uncontrollable physical movements. This code covers essential tremors (shaking that happens when trying to use your hands), tics, and dystonia (painful, involuntary muscle contractions that twist parts of the body).

name: Alzheimer disease
chapter: Chapter : VI Diseases of the nervous system
description: A progressive, irreversible brain disorder that slowly destroys memory, thinking skills, and eventually, the ability to carry out simple tasks. It is the most common cause of dementia.
inclusions: senile and presenile forms

name: Other Alzheimer disease
chapter: Chapter : VI Diseases of the nervous system
description: A progressive, irreversible brain disorder that slowly destroys memory, thinking skills, and eventually, the ability to carry out simple tasks. It is the most common cause of dementia.
inclusions: senile and presenile forms

name: Alzheimer disease, unspecified
chapter: Chapter : VI Diseases of the nervous system
description: A progressive, irreversible brain disorder that slowly destroys memory, thinking skills, and eventually, the ability to carry out simple tasks. It is the most common cause of dementia.     "Unspecified" Alzheimer's, used when a doctor diagnoses standard Alzheimer's without specifying if it is early-onset or late-onset.

name: Alzheimer disease, unspecified
chapter: Chapter : VI Diseases of the nervous system
description: A progressive, irreversible brain disorder that slowly destroys memory, thinking skills, and eventually, the ability to carry out simple tasks. It is the most common cause of dementia.     "Unspecified" Alzheimer's, used when a doctor diagnoses standard Alzheimer's without specifying if it is early-onset or late-onset.
inclusions: senile and presenile forms

name: Other degenerative diseases of nervous system, not elsewhere classified
chapter: Chapter : VI Diseases of the nervous system
description: Conditions where brain cells are dying, but the pattern doesn't match Alzheimer's or Parkinson's.The most common condition under this code is Frontotemporal Dementia (FTD). Unlike Alzheimer's, which hits memory first, FTD attacks the frontal and temporal lobes of the brain. This causes drastic changes in personality, loss of social filters, inappropriate behavior, or severe language difficulties, while memory often remains intact early on.

name: Multiple sclerosis
chapter: Chapter : VI Diseases of the nervous system
description: A chronic, often disabling disease where the body’s own immune system mistakenly attacks the Central Nervous System (the brain and spinal cord).Nerve fibers are insulated by a protective fatty coating called myelin (like the plastic insulation around an electrical wire). In MS, the immune system strips away this myelin. This leaves scar tissue (sclerosis) and disrupts or completely blocks the electrical signals traveling between the brain and the body. Symptoms vary wildly depending on which nerves are damaged, ranging from profound fatigue and numbness to vision loss, muscle weakness, and difficulty walking.
inclusions: Multiple sclerosis (of): NOS | Multiple sclerosis (of): brain stem | Multiple sclerosis (of): cord | Multiple sclerosis (of): disseminated | Multiple sclerosis (of): generalized

name: Other demyelinating diseases of central nervous system
chapter: Chapter : VI Diseases of the nervous system
description: Applied when the nerve insulation (myelin) is being destroyed, but the pattern or cause does not fit standard Multiple Sclerosis.What happens to the body: An example under this code is Devic's disease (Neuromyelitis Optica), where the immune system specifically targets the optic nerves and the spinal cord simultaneously. This causes rapid, severe inflammation resulting in sudden vision loss and temporary paralysis or weakness in the legs.

name: Epilepsy
chapter: Chapter : VI Diseases of the nervous system
description: A neurological disorder marked by a tendency to have unprovoked, repeated seizures. A single, isolated seizure (like one caused by a high fever) is not epilepsy; it must be a chronic, recurring issue to use this code. Seizures are caused by sudden, synchronized bursts of abnormal electrical activity in the brain. Think of it like an electrical storm or a temporary power surge in a computer. Depending on where the surge occurs, it can cause full-body convulsions (tonic-clonic seizures), brief periods of blank staring (absence seizures), or localized muscle jerking.

name: Status epilepticus
chapter: Chapter : VI Diseases of the nervous system
description: This is a critical medical emergency. It describes a seizure that lasts continuously for more than 5 minutes, or a series of seizures occurring so close together that the patient does not wake up between them.  During prolonged seizures, the brain is working at maximum capacity without a break, which rapidly depletes its oxygen and glucose supplies. If not stopped immediately with intravenous medications, it can lead to permanent brain damage, respiratory failure, or cardiac arrest due to the immense physical stress put on the body.

name: Transient cerebral ischaemic attacks and related syndromes
chapter: Chapter : VI Diseases of the nervous system
description: Commonly known as a "mini-stroke." It is a temporary period of stroke-like symptoms that resolves completely within 24 hours (usually within an hour) without leaving permanent brain damage.A tiny blood clot or a clump of cholesterol temporarily blocks an artery supplying blood to a specific part of the brain. Because the blood flow is cut off, the patient experiences sudden stroke symptoms: weakness on one side of the face, a numb arm, or slurred speech. The body's natural defense systems quickly dissolve the clot, restoring blood flow before the brain tissue actually dies. It is a critical warning sign that a major stroke may happen soon.

name: Carotid artery syndrome (hemispheric)
chapter: Chapter : VI Diseases of the nervous system
description: Commonly known as a "mini-stroke." It is a temporary period of stroke-like symptoms that resolves completely within 24 hours (usually within an hour) without leaving permanent brain damage.A tiny blood clot or a clump forms in the carotid arety, the massive blood vessels in the neck that supply the front of the brain. Because the blood flow is cut off, the patient experiences sudden stroke symptoms: weakness on one side of the face, a numb arm, or slurred speech. The body's natural defense systems quickly dissolve the clot, restoring blood flow before the brain tissue actually dies. It is a critical warning sign that a major stroke may happen soon.

name: Transient cerebral ischaemic attack, unspecified
chapter: Chapter : VI Diseases of the nervous system
description: Commonly known as a "mini-stroke." It is a temporary period of stroke-like symptoms that resolves completely within 24 hours (usually within an hour) without leaving permanent brain damage.A tiny blood clot or a clump of cholesterol temporarily blocks an artery supplying blood to a specific part of the brain. Because the blood flow is cut off, the patient experiences sudden stroke symptoms: weakness on one side of the face, a numb arm, or slurred speech. The body's natural defense systems quickly dissolve the clot, restoring blood flow before the brain tissue actually dies. It is a critical warning sign that a major stroke may happen soon. This Code does not specify where the heart artery blockage is located.
inclusions: Spasm of cerebral artery | Transient cerebral ischaemia NOS

name: Nerve root and plexus compressions in diseases classified elsewhere
chapter: Chapter : VI Diseases of the nervous system
description: This code is used when a nerve leaving the spinal cord is being physically pinched or crushed, but the underlying root cause is classified under a different medical chapter (such as a herniated disc, arthritis, or a bone tumor). When a nerve root is compressed as it exits the spine, the electrical signals get distorted. This results in radiculopathy—shooting pain, burning, numbness, or weakness that travels down the exact path of that nerve. A classic example is severe sciatica, where a pinched nerve in the lower back causes pain to shoot all the way down to the foot.

name: Mononeuropathy in diseases classified elsewhere
chapter: Chapter : VI Diseases of the nervous system
description: Damage localized to one single nerve anywhere in the body, caused by an underlying systemic illness. A prime example is diabetic mononeuropathy, where fluctuating blood sugar levels suddenly cut off the microscopic blood supply to one specific nerve. This often happens to the cranial nerves controlling the eye, causing a sudden droopy eyelid or double vision, or to a nerve in the wrist, causing sudden wrist drop.

name: Drug-induced polyneuropathy
chapter: Chapter : VI Diseases of the nervous system
description: Widespread, systemic damage affecting multiple peripheral nerves throughout the body simultaneously, usually starting in the longest nerves first. Instead of a single pinched nerve, thousands of microscopic nerve endings are slowly damaged. It almost always begins in the toes and feet before moving up the legs (often called a "stocking-glove" pattern). It causes constant burning pain, tingling ("pins and needles"), and a loss of sensation that makes patients feel like they are walking on cotton or pins.This code General or idiopathic polyneuropathy (such as nerve damage caused by chronic alcoholism, nutritional deficiencies, or unknown reasons).

name: Polyneuropathy in infectious and parasitic diseases classified elsewhere
chapter: Chapter : VI Diseases of the nervous system
description: Widespread, systemic damage affecting multiple peripheral nerves throughout the body simultaneously, usually starting in the longest nerves first. Instead of a single pinched nerve, thousands of microscopic nerve endings are slowly damaged. It almost always begins in the toes and feet before moving up the legs (often called a "stocking-glove" pattern). It causes constant burning pain, tingling ("pins and needles"), and a loss of sensation that makes patients feel like they are walking on cotton or pins.This code explanses Nerve damage caused directly by another officially diagnosed disease, most commonly Diabetic Peripheral Neuropathy.

name: Hemiplegia
chapter: Chapter : VI Diseases of the nervous system
description: Paralysis that completely freezes or severely weakens one vertical half of the body (left side or right side).This is rarely a problem with the muscles themselves; it is an injury to the opposite side of the brain. If a stroke or injury damages the motor cortex on the left side of the brain, the right side of the body becomes paralyzed.

name: Hemiplegia, unspecified
chapter: Chapter : VI Diseases of the nervous system
description: Paralysis that completely freezes or severely weakens one vertical half of the body (left side or right side).This is rarely a problem with the muscles themselves; it is an injury to the opposite side of the brain. If a stroke or injury damages the motor cortex on the left side of the brain, the right side of the body becomes paralyzed.

name: Paraplegia and tetraplegia
chapter: Chapter : VI Diseases of the nervous system
description: Severe paralysis affecting the lower half of the body (paraplegia) or all four limbs (tetraplegia, also known as quadriplegia). This is typically caused by a spinal cord injury or disease. The spinal cord acts as the main highway for brain signals. If the highway is severed or crushed in the lower back, signals cannot reach the legs (paraplegia). If the injury occurs high up in the neck, the signals are cut off from the arms, chest, and legs (tetraplegia), requiring long-term mobility and respiratory assistance.

name: Diplegia of upper limbs
chapter: Chapter : VI Diseases of the nervous system
description: Forms of paralysis that do not fit the clean definitions of one-sided or half-body paralysis. This includes monoplegia (paralysis of just one single limb, like a single arm after a focal nerve injury) or Cauda Equina Syndrome, where the bundle of nerves at the very base of the spine is suddenly crushed, causing a loss of bladder control and numbness in the groin area.
inclusions: paralysis (complete)(incomplete), except as in G80-G82

name: Other disorders of brain
chapter: Chapter : VI Diseases of the nervous system
description: A broad category for structural or metabolic brain injuries that aren't caused by a stroke or direct physical trauma (like a car accident). This code is frequently used for Anoxic Brain Injury (when the brain is starved of oxygen, such as during a near-drowning or cardiac arrest) or Cerebral Edema (dangerous swelling of the brain tissue inside the skull). Because the skull cannot expand, any swelling puts intense pressure on the brain, cutting off healthy blood flow and damaging vital regulatory centers.

name: Other diseases of spinal cord
chapter: Chapter : VI Diseases of the nervous system
description: This code is used for progressive or acute damage to the spinal cord that is not caused by sudden physical trauma (like a car crash, fall, or sports injury). It covers structural, inflammatory, and vascular issues within the spinal column. The spinal cord is the main electrical cable carrying signals between your brain and your body. When it is compressed or inflamed by non-traumatic diseases, those signals get blocked or jumbled. Common conditions falling under this code include: Myelopathy: Gradual compression of the spinal cord, often due to severe arthritis or narrowing of the spinal canal (spinal stenosis).Myelitis: Sudden inflammation of the spinal cord tissue, often triggered by a viral infection. This disrupts the protective myelin coating around the nerves.Spinal Cord Infarction: A "stroke" inside the spinal cord, where a blood clot cuts off the blood supply to the nerve tissue, causing rapid cell death.Common Symptoms:Stiff, weak, or heavy legs making it difficult to walk or maintain balance.Loss of fine motor skills in the hands, like struggling to button a shirt.Numbness, tingling, or a "band-like" tightness around the chest or abdomen.Sudden or gradual loss of bowel or bladder control.

name: Other disorders of nervous system in diseases classified elsewhere
chapter: Chapter : VI Diseases of the nervous system
description: This is a "dual-coding" or "manifestation" code. It means the patient's nervous system is being actively damaged, but the root cause is a completely different underlying medical condition that belongs to another area of medicine (like cancer, metabolic diseases, or endocrine issues).What happens to the body: This code is never used on its own; it is always paired with the primary illness. The underlying disease creates toxins, causes nutritional starvation, or physically spreads to damage healthy nerve tissue. Common examples include:Autonomic Neuropathy in Cancer: A tumor or cancer treatment damages the involuntary nervous system, causing sudden dangerous drops in blood pressure when standing up.Nutritional Nervous System Damage: Severe, long-term malnutrition or vitamin deficiencies (like a total lack of Vitamin B12) causing the spinal cord tracts to slowly degenerate.Amyloidosis of the Nervous System: A rare disease where abnormal proteins build up in peripheral nerves, destroying their ability to send messages.Common Symptoms: Symptoms depend entirely on which nerves are targeted by the underlying disease, but they frequently include:Severe, unexplained nerve pain or numbness.Dizziness, fainting, or rapid heart rate due to misfiring involuntary nerves.Progressive muscle wasting and unexplained physical weakness.

name: Other disorders of eyelid
chapter: Chapter :	VII Diseases of the eye and adnexa
description: Covers structural eyelid failures, shifted lenses, gel breakdowns inside the eye, localized eyeball infections, symptoms like double-vision, or surgical complications from eye procedures.

name: Senile cataract
chapter: Chapter :	VII Diseases of the eye and adnexa
description: A gradual clouding of the eye’s natural, internal lens, which sits right behind the pupil. The lens is made mostly of water and precisely arranged proteins that keep it perfectly clear to let light pass through. Over time, these proteins begin to clump together, turning the clear lens yellowish, brownish, or cloudy. It is like looking through a foggy, smeared window, making it difficult to read, drive, or see colors clearly.

name: Other cataract
chapter: Chapter :	VII Diseases of the eye and adnexa
description: A gradual clouding of the eye’s natural, internal lens, which sits right behind the pupil. The lens is made mostly of water and precisely arranged proteins that keep it perfectly clear to let light pass through. Over time, these proteins begin to clump together, turning the clear lens yellowish, brownish, or cloudy. It is like looking through a foggy, smeared window, making it difficult to read, drive, or see colors clearly.

name: Other disorders of lens
chapter: Chapter :	VII Diseases of the eye and adnexa
description: Covers structural eyelid failures, shifted lenses, gel breakdowns inside the eye, localized eyeball infections, symptoms like double-vision, or surgical complications from eye procedures.

name: Retinal detachments and breaks
chapter: Chapter :	VII Diseases of the eye and adnexa
description: A time-sensitive, vision-threatening emergency where the retina (the light-sensitive layer of tissue at the back of the eye) pulls away from its underlying supportive layer of blood vessels. If a small tear forms in the retina, the fluid inside the eyeball can leak underneath it, acting like water lifting wallpaper off a wall. As the retina detaches, it is starved of oxygen and nutrients. Patients describe seeing sudden flashes of light, a shower of dark "floaters," or a dark shadow/curtain pulling across their field of vision. If not surgically reattached quickly, it causes permanent blindness in that eye.

name: Other retinal disorders
chapter: Chapter :	VII Diseases of the eye and adnexa
description: Chronic damage to the retina that alters how visual information is captured and sent to the brain.H35 includes conditions like Macular Degeneration, where the central part of the retina deteriorates, wiping out a person's straight-ahead vision while leaving their side vision intact.

name: Retinal disorders in diseases classified elsewhere
chapter: Chapter :	VII Diseases of the eye and adnexa
description: Chronic damage to the retina that alters how visual information is captured and sent to the brain.H36 is reserved for retinal damage caused by outside illnesses—most notably Diabetic Retinopathy, where high blood sugar weakens the tiny blood vessels in the eye, causing them to leak fluid, bleed into the eye, and grow abnormal, fragile new vessels that distort vision.

name: Glaucoma
chapter: Chapter :	VII Diseases of the eye and adnexa
description: A disease known as the "silent thief of sight" because it slowly steals vision without any pain or early warning signs. It causes progressive damage to the optic nerve.  The front of the eye constantly produces a clear fluid that needs to drain out. If the drainage channels get clogged or narrowed, fluid builds up, causing the pressure inside the eyeball (intraocular pressure) to spike. This elevated pressure physically crushes the delicate nerve fibers of the optic nerve at the back of the eye, slowly wiping out peripheral (side) vision first until only a narrow tunnel of vision remains.

name: Disorders of vitreous body
chapter: Chapter :	VII Diseases of the eye and adnexa
description: Covers structural eyelid failures, shifted lenses, gel breakdowns inside the eye, localized eyeball infections, symptoms like double-vision, or surgical complications from eye procedures.

name: Disorders of globe
chapter: Chapter :	VII Diseases of the eye and adnexa
description: Covers structural eyelid failures, shifted lenses, gel breakdowns inside the eye, localized eyeball infections, symptoms like double-vision, or surgical complications from eye procedures.
inclusions: disorders affecting multiple structures of eye

name: Visual disturbances
chapter: Chapter :	VII Diseases of the eye and adnexa
description: Covers structural eyelid failures, shifted lenses, gel breakdowns inside the eye, localized eyeball infections, symptoms like double-vision, or surgical complications from eye procedures.

name: Visual impairment including blindness (binocular or monocular)
chapter: Chapter :	VII Diseases of the eye and adnexa
description: An administrative and clinical code used to document legal blindness or profound visual impairment that cannot be corrected with regular glasses, contact lenses, or surgery. This code tracks severity scales rather than a specific disease mechanism. It tells insurers and health agencies exactly how limited a person's eyesight is—ranging from severe low vision (unable to read large print) to total blindness (no light perception at all) in one or both eyes.

name: Postprocedural disorders of eye and adnexa, not elsewhere classified
chapter: Chapter :	VII Diseases of the eye and adnexa
description: Covers structural eyelid failures, shifted lenses, gel breakdowns inside the eye, localized eyeball infections, symptoms like double-vision, or surgical complications from eye procedures.

name: Other disorders of external ear
chapter: Diseases of the ear and mastoid process
description: Outer ear mechanical issues (like severe earwax blockages causing pressure) and clinical hearing loss (nerve-related or mechanical deafness).

name: Other hearing loss
chapter: Diseases of the ear and mastoid process
description: Outer ear mechanical issues (like severe earwax blockages causing pressure) and clinical hearing loss (nerve-related or mechanical deafness).

name: Multiple valve diseases
inclusions: whether specified as rheumatic or of unspecified origin

name: Essential (primary) hypertension
description: Chronic, long-term high blood pressure that develops gradually over years with no single, identifiable medical cause (like a kidney tumor or a medication side effect). It affects roughly 1 in 3 adults. Blood pressure is the force of your blood pushing against the walls of your arteries. In hypertension, the heart has to work significantly harder to pump blood because the blood vessels have become stiff, narrow, or restricted. It causes quiet, microscopic wear and tear to blood vessels across the entire body, dramatically increasing the risk of strokes and heart attacks over time.
inclusions: High blood pressure | Hypertension (arterial)(benign)(essential)(malignant)(primary)(systemic)

name: Hypertensive heart disease
description: Structural damage to the heart muscle that happens as a direct consequence of years of untreated or poorly controlled high blood pressure (I10).Because the heart has been forcing blood through stiff, high-pressure arteries for years, the muscle adapts to the heavy workload by thickening—a condition called Left Ventricular Hypertrophy (similar to how a bicep grows larger from lifting heavy weights). However, a thickened heart muscle becomes stiff, less efficient, and struggles to relax between beats. Over time, this leads directly to congestive heart failure, where the heart can no longer pump enough blood to meet the body's needs, causing fluid to back up into the lungs and legs.
inclusions: any condition in I50.-, I51.4-I51.9 due to hypertension

name: Hypertensive renal disease
description: Permanent, progressive kidney damage and scarring caused entirely by long-term high blood pressure. The kidneys filter waste out of your blood using millions of microscopic, delicate blood vessel loops called glomeruli. High blood pressure acts like a pressure-washer inside these tiny, fragile filters. Over time, the intense pressure scars and destroys these vessels. As the filters fail, the kidneys lose their ability to clean the blood, leading to a dangerous buildup of toxins in the body (uremia) and potentially requiring long-term dialysis or a kidney transplant.
inclusions: any condition in N00-N07, N18.-, N19 or N26 due to hypertension | arteriosclerosis of kidney | arteriosclerotic nephritis (chronic)(interstitial) | hypertensive nephropathy | nephrosclerosis

name: Hypertensive renal disease with renal failure
description: iltering units of the kidneys (the glomeruli) [I12]. Over many years, this pressure causes extensive scarring. When it reaches Stage 5 or End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD), the kidneys lose their ability to filter waste products, balance fluids, and remove excess water from the blood. Toxins build up rapidly in the bloodstream (uremia) [I12], which can affect other organs like the brain and heart. At this stage, the body requires mechanical filtration or surgery to survive.Common Symptoms: Severe fluid retention (swelling in the legs, ankles, or face), profound fatigue, nausea, confusion, a metallic taste in the mouth, and short breath.Typical Management Options: Long-term dialysis (hemodialysis or peritoneal dialysis), strict fluid and dietary restrictions, or a kidney transplant.
inclusions: Hypertensive renal failure

name: Angina pectoris
description: This is the clinical term for chest pain or discomfort that happens when your heart muscle does not get enough oxygen-rich blood. It is a major warning sign of coronary artery disease. Cholesterol and fatty plaques build up inside the coronary arteries (the blood vessels that feed the heart muscle), narrowing the pathway. When a person rests, the heart can usually get by on the limited blood flow. However, during exertion, stress, or cold weather, the heart beats faster and demands more oxygen. Because the narrowed arteries cannot deliver enough blood, the heart muscle temporarily starves for oxygen, causing an ache or cramping sensation.Common Symptoms: A squeezing, pressure, heaviness, or tight pain in the center of the chest. This pain can radiate out to the left shoulder, arm, neck, or jaw. It is usually triggered by physical activity and goes away within minutes of resting or taking medication.Typical Management Options: Medications like nitroglycerin (to rapidly widen the blood vessels), beta-blockers, lifestyle changes, or procedures like placing a stent (angioplasty) to prop the artery open.

name: Acute myocardial infarction
description: This is the formal medical term for a heart attack. It is a time-sensitive, life-threatening medical emergency. Unlike angina, where blood flow is just restricted, a heart attack happens when a fatty plaque inside a coronary artery suddenly ruptures. The body tries to heal the tear by forming a blood clot over it. This clot completely blocks the artery, completely cutting off the blood and oxygen supply to a specific section of the heart muscle. Within minutes, the starved heart muscle cells begin to die. If the artery is not cleared quickly, that part of the heart suffers permanent, irreversible muscle damage or scarring, which can stop the heart entirely.Common Symptoms: Severe, crushing chest pain or pressure that does not go away with rest or medication. It is frequently accompanied by sudden shortness of breath, breaking out in a cold sweat, dizziness, extreme anxiety, and unexplained nausea or vomiting.Typical Management Options: Immediate emergency medical attention. Emergency interventions include blood-thinning or clot-busting medications, an immediate cardiac catheterization to open the blocked vessel with a balloon and stent, or coronary artery bypass surgery.
inclusions: myocardial infarction specified as acute or with a stated duration of 4 weeks (28 days) or less from onset

name: Acute transmural myocardial infarction of anterior wall
description: A severe heart attack where a major artery supplying the front (anterior) wall of the heart is completely blocked. "STEMI" means a specific, dangerous pattern is visible on an EKG.The front wall of the left ventricle is the heart's main pumping powerhouse. When its blood supply is entirely cut off, a massive area of critical muscle tissue immediately begins to die. This rapidly weakens the heart's ability to pump blood to the rest of the body
inclusions: Transmural infarction (acute)(of): anterior (wall) NOS | Transmural infarction (acute)(of): anteroapical | Transmural infarction (acute)(of): anterolateral | Transmural infarction (acute)(of): anteroseptal

name: Acute myocardial infarction
description: A sub-category of the front-wall heart attack, specifying a total blockage in the Left Main Coronary Artery. In medicine, this is often called the "widowmaker" block. The left main artery is the trunk line that feeds the entire left side of the heart. A complete blockage here threatens a vast portion of the heart muscle at once, making it a critical, high-mortality medical emergency.
inclusions: myocardial infarction specified as acute or with a stated duration of 4 weeks (28 days) or less from onset

name: Acute transmural myocardial infarction of inferior wall
description: A total artery blockage causing a heart attack on the bottom (inferior/diaphragmatic) wall of the heart.The bottom of the heart sits right above the diaphragm. While still dangerous, an inferior STEMI typically affects a smaller portion of the primary pumping muscle than an anterior wall attack. However, it frequently disrupts the heart's natural electrical pacemaker node, which sits near this area.
inclusions: Transmural infarction (acute)(of): diaphragmatic wall | Transmural infarction (acute)(of): inferior (wall) NOS | Transmural infarction (acute)(of): inferolateral | Transmural infarction (acute)(of): inferoposterior

name: Acute myocardial infarction
description: A total artery blockage causing a heart attack on the bottom (inferior/diaphragmatic) wall of the heart.The bottom of the heart sits right above the diaphragm. While still dangerous, an inferior STEMI typically affects a smaller portion of the primary pumping muscle than an anterior wall attack. However, it frequently disrupts the heart's natural electrical pacemaker node, which sits near this area.
inclusions: myocardial infarction specified as acute or with a stated duration of 4 weeks (28 days) or less from onset

name: Acute myocardial infarction
description: A specific code identifying that the inferior heart attack was caused by a total blockage in the Right Coronary Artery (RCA). This artery supplies blood to the right side of the heart and the electrical system.
inclusions: myocardial infarction specified as acute or with a stated duration of 4 weeks (28 days) or less from onset

name: Acute transmural myocardial infarction of other sites
description: A total artery blockage causing a heart attack in other specific regions of the heart muscle, such as the lateral (side) wall or posterior (back) wall. [1]
inclusions: Transmural infarction (acute)(of): apical-lateral | Transmural infarction (acute)(of): basal-lateral | Transmural infarction (acute)(of): high lateral | Transmural infarction (acute)(of): lateral (wall) NOS | Transmural infarction (acute)(of): posterior (true) | Transmural infarction (acute)(of): posterobasal | Transmural infarction (acute)(of): posterolateral | Transmural infarction (acute)(of): posteroseptal | Transmural infarction (acute)(of): septal NOS

name: Acute transmural myocardial infarction of unspecified site
description: The medical documentation confirms a complete blockage heart attack (STEMI) happened, but the specific wall or artery location was not recorded by the physician
inclusions: Transmural myocardial infarction NOS

name: Acute subendocardial myocardial infarction
description: A Non-ST Elevation Myocardial Infarction (NSTEMI).Unlike a STEMI where an artery is 100% blocked, an NSTEMI usually involves a partially blocked or severely narrowed artery. Blood can still trickle through, but it is not enough. The damage is restricted to the innermost layer of the heart muscle tissue (the subendocardium) rather than tearing through the entire thickness of the heart wall.
inclusions: Myocardial infarction with non-ST elevation | Nontransmural myocardial infarction NOS

name: Acute myocardial infarction, unspecified
description: A general billing code used when a patient definitively had a sudden heart attack, but the medical record lacks the details to state whether it was a STEMI or an NSTEMI
inclusions: Myocardial infarction (acute) NOS

name: Acute myocardial infarction
description: A general billing code used when a patient definitively had a sudden heart attack, but the medical record lacks the details to state whether it was a STEMI or an NSTEMI
inclusions: myocardial infarction specified as acute or with a stated duration of 4 weeks (28 days) or less from onset

name: Hypertensive chronic kidney disease without renal failure, with hypertensive urgency
description: The code means a patient has long-term, high blood pressure that has already caused permanent damage or scarring to the kidneys. Additionally, during this specific clinical visit, the patient's blood pressure has dramatically spiked to dangerously high levels (typically 180/120 mmHg or higher).
inclusions: myocardial infarction specified as acute or with a stated duration of 4 weeks (28 days) or less from onset

name: Acute myocardial infarction
description: A general billing code used when a patient definitively had a sudden heart attack, but the medical record lacks the details to state whether it was a STEMI or an NSTEMI
inclusions: myocardial infarction specified as acute or with a stated duration of 4 weeks (28 days) or less from onset

name: Subsequent myocardial infarction
description: The patient is already recovering from an initial heart attack, but within 28 days, a new blockage forms and causes a second heart attack on the front wall of the heart.
inclusions: myocardial infarction: extension | myocardial infarction: recurrent | myocardial infarction: reinfarction

name: Subsequent myocardial infarction of anterior wall
description: The patient is already recovering from an initial heart attack, but within 28 days, a new blockage forms and causes a second heart attack on the front wall of the heart.
inclusions: Subsequent infarction (acute)(of): anterior (wall) NOS | Subsequent infarction (acute)(of): anteroapical | Subsequent infarction (acute)(of): anterolateral | Subsequent infarction (acute)(of): anteroseptal

name: Subsequent myocardial infarction of unspecified site
description: A repeat heart attack occurring within the 4-week window, but the specific location of the second blockage is unrecorded.

name: Other acute ischaemic heart diseases
description: Sudden, severe heart complications where blood flow is critically dropped, but it does not cleanly result in a textbook heart attack. This includes conditions like Dressler's syndrome (inflammation around the heart after an attack) or coronary microvascular spasms.
