name: Chronic ischaemic heart disease
description: Long-term, slow-progressing Coronary Artery Disease (CAD).What happens to the body: Over decades, cholesterol plaques slowly build up and harden inside the heart's arteries (atherosclerosis). The heart adapts to a permanent state of reduced blood flow, causing chronic shortness of breath during activities or stable angina (predictable chest pain).

name: Old myocardial infarction
description: An administrative code used to note a past history of a heart attack. It means the patient had a heart attack a long time ago (more than 4 weeks ago), the tissue has already healed into scar tissue, and it is no longer an active medical emergency. [1, 2]
inclusions: Healed myocardial infarction | Past myocardial infarction diagnosed by ECG or other special investigation, but currently presenting no symptoms

name: Pulmonary embolism
description: A sudden, life-threatening blockage in the blood vessels of the lungs, not the heart. A blood clot forms somewhere else in the body (usually deep inside a leg vein, known as a Deep Vein Thrombosis or DVT). The clot breaks loose, travels through the bloodstream, passes through the right side of the heart, and gets physically wedged inside the main pulmonary arteries of the lungs. This blocks blood from picking up oxygen.
inclusions: pulmonary (artery)(vein): infarction | pulmonary (artery)(vein): thromboembolism | pulmonary (artery)(vein): thrombosis

name: Other pulmonary heart diseases
description: Chronic conditions where a long-term lung disease causes structural failures inside the heart.Conditions like severe COPD or pulmonary hypertension scar the lungs, making it very difficult for the heart to push blood through them. The right ventricle of the heart has to pump against massive resistance, causing it to enlarge, stretch out, and eventually fail (a condition called Cor Pulmonale).

name: Other diseases of pericardium
description: A category for structural problems with the heart sac that aren't sudden infections. The most common condition here is a pericardial effusion (excess fluid buildup inside the sac). If too much fluid accumulates inside the rigid pericardial sac, it begins to physically compress the heart. In severe cases, this leads to a life-threatening crisis called cardiac tamponade, where the heart is squeezed so tightly that it cannot expand fully to fill with blood, causing blood pressure to plummet.

name: Acute and subacute endocarditis
description: Active inflammation of the heart sac caused entirely by an underlying systemic illness. Instead of a simple viral infection, an autoimmune disease (like Lupus or Rheumatoid Arthritis) or a cancer causes the immune system to attack the heart sac. The two layers of the sac become inflamed and rub against each other like sandpaper every time the heart beats.

name: Nonrheumatic mitral valve disorders
description: Malfunctions of the mitral valve (the valve separating the left upper and lower chambers) not caused by historic rheumatic fever. Covers mitral valve prolapse (the valve flaps bulge backward like a loose parachute) or mitral regurgitation (the valve leaks, causing blood to flow backward into the lungs instead of moving forward to the body). This forces the heart to work twice as hard to pump the same amount of blood.

name: Nonrheumatic aortic valve disorders
description: Malfunctions of the aortic valve (the main exit gate from the heart to the rest of the body).Commonly covers aortic stenosis, where the valve becomes stiff, calcified, and narrowed. The heart muscle must generate immense, dangerous pressure to force blood through the tiny opening, causing the muscle to thicken and eventually tire out.

name: Dilated cardiomyopathy
description: A chronic disease where the heart's main pumping chamber (the left ventricle) becomes enlarged, stretched out, and structurally weakened.Think of the heart muscle stretching out like an overused rubber band. Because the heart walls become thin and floppy, the muscle cannot squeeze forcefully enough to push blood out to the rest of the body. This causes blood to back up into the lungs and liver, eventually leading to congestive heart failure. It also stretches the internal electrical wiring, triggering dangerous heart rhythms.
inclusions: Congestive cardiomyopathy

name: Atrioventricular and left bundle-branch block
description: An electrical "traffic jam" or total blockage in the wiring that coordinates the top and bottom chambers of the heart. (This was the final code on your previous list, marking a slowed or disrupted electrical pulse).

name: Other conduction disorders
description: A broad category for other specific electrical wiring defects in the heart, such as Right Bundle Branch Block (RBBB) or pre-excitation syndromes (like Wolff-Parkinson-White).The electrical signal traveling through the heart muscle is forced to take a detour because a specific pathway is damaged. This causes one ventricle to squeeze a fraction of a second later than the other, making the heart pump less efficiently.

name: Cardiac arrest
description: A sudden, life-threatening medical emergency where the heart completely stops beating out of nowhere. It is an electrical failure, distinct from a heart attack (which is a plumbing/circulation blockage).: The heart's electrical system completely misfires, causing the heart to suddenly stop pumping blood. Within seconds, blood stops flowing to the brain, lungs, and vital organs. The person immediately loses consciousness and stops breathing. Without immediate CPR and a shock from a defibrillator (AED), death occurs within minutes.

name: Paroxysmal tachycardia
description: Sudden, episodic bursts of an extremely fast heart rate (usually over 100 to 250 beats per minute) that start and stop abruptly. An abnormal electrical short-circuit in the upper or lower chambers takes over the heart's natural pacemaker. Because the heart is beating so rapidly, the chambers do not have enough time to fill up with blood between beats, causing blood pressure to drop.

name: Atrial fibrillation and flutter
description: The most common chronic heart rhythm disorder. It causes the upper chambers of the heart (the atria) to beat completely irregularly and chaotically. Instead of a single, coordinated electrical squeeze, the top chambers twitch or quiver chaotically (fibrillate). This prevents blood from being pumped smoothly into the lower chambers, causing blood to pool in the crevices of the heart. If pooled blood forms a clot, it can be pumped straight up to the brain, causing an ischemic stroke.

name: Heart failure
description: The overarching category code for a heart that can no longer keep up with the body's demands for oxygen and blood.

name: Congestive heart failure
description: The general baseline code used when a patient has a buildup of fluid in the body (congestion) due to heart weakness, but the doctor hasn't specified if it is the left or right side failing.
inclusions: Congestive heart disease | Right ventricular failure (secondary to left heart failure)

name: Heart failure
description: Used for specific, less common sub-types of fluid-overload heart failure, such as high-output heart failure.

name: Left ventricular failure
description: Failure of the left side of the heart, which is responsible for pumping oxygen-rich blood out to the entire body. When the left ventricle fails to pump blood forward, the fluid gets backed up directly into the lungs. This causes fluid to leak into the lung tissue (pulmonary edema), essentially drowning the air sacs.
inclusions: Cardiac asthma | Left heart failure | Oedema of lung with mention of heart disease NOS or heart failure | Pulmonary oedema with mention of heart disease NOS or heart failure

name: Heart failure
description: Left-sided heart failure where the physician has not specified if the muscle is failing to squeeze (systolic) or failing to relax (diastolic).

name: Heart failure
description: Clinically known as Systolic Heart Failure. The heart muscle has become noticeably stretched, thin, and weak. The "ejection fraction" measures the percentage of blood pumped out with each beat; in this condition, that percentage drops dangerously low (often below 40%).

name: Heart failure
description: Left-sided heart failure variants, including Diastolic Heart Failure (where the muscle is thick and stiff, meaning it can squeeze fine but cannot relax to fill with enough blood).

name: Heart failure, unspecified
description: A broad administrative code used when a patient is actively being treated for heart failure, but the medical records lack specific diagnostic tests (like an echocardiogram) to pinpoint the exact chamber or mechanism.
inclusions: Cardiac, heart or myocardial failure NOS

name: Heart failure
description: A highly generalized billing code indicating heart failure is present, but missing all structural sub-details.

name: Heart failure
description: A secondary catch-all for atypical heart failure manifestations that do not meet standard left or right definitions.

name: Complications and ill-defined descriptions of heart disease
description: A broad category used for generic heart issues that aren't fully diagnosed yet, such as an enlarged heart (cardiomegaly) or a ruptured intracardiac structure.

name: Subarachnoid haemorrhage
description: A catastrophic medical emergency involving a specific type of stroke where there is life-threatening bleeding into the space surrounding the brain. A blood vessel on the surface of the brain (often a brain aneurysm) suddenly bursts. Blood floods into the subarachnoid space—the area between the brain and the thin skull tissues where cerebrospinal fluid flows. The sudden bleeding causes a massive, dangerous spike in pressure inside the skull, compressing vital brain structures and cutting off oxygen.

name: Subarachnoid haemorrhage, unspecified
description: A severe, life-threatening emergency where a blood vessel bursts and leaks blood into the protective space surrounding the brain, without the exact source artery being specified.

name: Intracerebral haemorrhage
description: A stroke caused by a blood vessel bursting directly inside the brain tissue itself.Chronic high blood pressure often weakens the small arteries deep within the brain until one ruptures. The leaking blood pools rapidly, forming a clot (hematoma) that physically compresses, crushes, and starves the surrounding brain cells of oxygen.

name: Intracerebral haemorrhage, unspecified
description: A stroke caused by bleeding inside the brain tissue where the medical record does not specify the exact localized artery or hemisphere.

name: Other nontraumatic intracranial haemorrhage
description: A category for other types of brain bleeding that are not caused by direct physical trauma (like a car crash or a fall). This includes conditions like subdural or epidural hematomas that happen spontaneously due to blood thinners or weak blood vessels.

name: Nontraumatic subdural haemorrhage
description: Bleeding that pools in the space between the brain's outer protective membrane (the dura) and the brain itself, occurring without an external injury. It is common in older adults or individuals taking strong blood thinners, where fragile veins tear and slowly leak blood over days or weeks.

name: Cerebral infarction
description: The formal medical term for an acute ischemic stroke. It is a time-sensitive emergency.
inclusions: occlusion and stenosis of cerebral and precerebral arteries (including truncus brachiocephalicus), resulting in cerebral infarction

name: Cerebral infarction due to unspecified occlusion or stenosis of precerebral arteries
description: A stroke caused by severe narrowing (stenosis) or total blockage (occlusion) in multiple main arteries in the neck that lead up to the brain (such as both carotid arteries or the vertebral arteries).

name: Other cerebral infarction
description: An ischemic stroke caused by less common or unusual mechanisms, such as a sudden tear in the lining of a brain artery (arterial dissection).

name: Cerebral infarction, unspecified
description: The most frequently used stroke code. It means a patient definitively suffered a standard stroke caused by a blood clot, but the exact artery location or the root source of the clot was not specified.

name: Stroke, not specified as haemorrhage or infarction
description: A highly generalized, emergency baseline code. It means a patient is actively suffering an acute stroke, but it is unspecified whether it is being caused by a bleed or a blood clot (usually used before a brain scan can be completed).
inclusions: Cerebrovascular accident NOS

name: Stroke, not specified as haemorrhage or infarction
description: A highly generalized, emergency baseline code. It means a patient is actively suffering an acute stroke, but it is unspecified whether it is being caused by a bleed or a blood clot (usually used before a brain scan can be completed).
inclusions: Cerebrovascular accident NOS

name: Occlusion and stenosis of precerebral arteries, not resulting in cerebral infarction
description: A main artery in the neck is heavily narrowed or blocked, but the body has managed to avoid a full stroke.
inclusions: embolism of basilar, carotid or vertebral arteries, not resulting in cerebral infarction | narrowing of basilar, carotid or vertebral arteries, not resulting in cerebral infarction | obstruction (complete)(partial) of basilar, carotid or vertebral arteries, not resulting in cerebral infarction | thrombosis of basilar, carotid or vertebral arteries, not resulting in cerebral infarction

name: Occlusion and stenosis of carotid artery
description: Severe plaque buildup or a partial blockage inside the carotid artery (the massive pulse point in your neck that supplies the front of the brain). It is a major warning sign for future strokes.

name: Occlusion and stenosis of cerebral arteries, not resulting in cerebral infarction
description: Critical narrowing or blockage in the arteries inside the brain itself, without currently causing a full stroke.
inclusions: embolism of middle, anterior and posterior cerebral arteries, and cerebellar arteries, not resulting in cerebral infarction | narrowing of middle, anterior and posterior cerebral arteries, and cerebellar arteries, not resulting in cerebral infarction | obstruction (complete)(partial) of middle, anterior and posterior cerebral arteries, and cerebellar arteries, not resulting in cerebral infarction | thrombosis of middle, anterior and posterior cerebral arteries, and cerebellar arteries, not resulting in cerebral infarction

name: Other cerebrovascular diseases
description: A  family of chronic, non-acute conditions affecting the blood vessels of the brain.

name: Other specified cerebrovascular diseases
description: Includes conditions like Acute Hypertensive Encephalopathy (where massive blood pressure spikes cause brain swelling) or cerebral ischemia.
inclusions: Acute cerebrovascular insufficiency NOS | Cerebral ischaemia (chronic)

name: Cerebrovascular disease, unspecified
description: A general diagnostic label indicating long-term, chronic damage or hardening of the brain's blood vessels (such as cerebral arteriosclerosis).

name: Sequelae of cerebrovascular disease
description: A highly important administrative category used to track the long-term aftereffects or disabilities left behind after a past stroke. This code is used months or years after a stroke has healed. It is paired with codes for lasting challenges, such as aphasia (difficulty speaking), hemiplegia (paralysis on one side of the body), or cognitive changes caused by the historical brain injury.

name: Sequelae of subarachnoid haemorrhage
description: The long-term aftereffects, disabilities, or chronic neurological deficits left behind following a past bleed in the space surrounding the brain. This code is used months or years after the initial emergency has resolved to track permanent issues like chronic headaches, speech difficulties, or cognitive changes caused by the historical injury.

name: Sequelae of cerebral infarction
description: The chronic, lasting aftereffects resulting from a past stroke caused by a blood clot (ischemic stroke).

name: Sequelae of stroke, not specified as haemorrhage or infarction
description: Broadly known as "hardening of the arteries." Cholesterol, fats, and calcium slowly build up inside the walls of the arteries, forming hard plaques. This narrows the pathways, restricts oxygen-rich blood flow to the body's limbs and organs, and causes the blood vessels to lose their natural elasticity.

name: Atherosclerosis
description: Hardening and plaque buildup specifically inside the aorta, which is the massive primary artery that carries all oxygen-rich blood away from the heart to the rest of the body.
inclusions: arteriolosclerosis | arteriosclerosis | arteriosclerotic vascular disease | atheroma | degeneration: arterial | degeneration: arteriovascular | degeneration: vascular | endarteritis deformans or obliterans | senile: arteritis | senile: endarteritis

name: Aortic aneurysm and dissection
description: A dangerous structural failure in the wall of the aorta.An aneurysm is a balloon-like bulge where the artery wall has worn thin and weak under high pressure. A dissection is a medical emergency where the inner layer of the aorta tears, allowing blood to surge between the layers of the vessel wall and physically split them apart.

name: Abdominal aortic aneurysm, ruptured
description: This is a catastrophic, time-sensitive medical emergency. It means the large, ballooned section of the aorta passing through the belly has physically burst open. The aorta is the largest artery in the human body and carries blood directly out of the heart under very high pressure. When an abdominal aneurysm ruptures, blood floods rapidly out of the circulatory system and into the abdominal cavity. The body's blood pressure drops instantly and drastically, starving the brain and vital organs of oxygen. Without immediate emergency trauma surgery to clamp the vessel, it leads to rapid, fatal internal bleeding.

name: Abdominal aortic aneurysm, without mention of rupture
description: This code means a patient has a known, structural ballooning bulge in the lower part of their aorta, but it is currently intact and has not burst.Due to years of high blood pressure or genetics, the middle layer of the aortic wall becomes weak, thin, and stretched out like an over-inflated tire. As long as it remains unruptured, blood continues to flow through it normally, but it must be monitored meticulously because as the diameter grows, the risk of a sudden rupture increases.

name: Other aneurysm and dissection
description: This is a broad category code used for a structural bulge (aneurysm) or an internal layer tear (dissection) occurring in any major artery of the body outside of the aorta or brain.Just like the aorta, other high-pressure arteries can wear thin and bulge or tear. This category tracks focal arterial blowouts in localized spots, such as:I72.3: Iliac artery (supplying the pelvis).I72.4: Popliteal artery (the major artery behind the knee—very common and can cause blood clots that cut off flow to the foot).I72.2: Renal artery (the blood line directly feeding the kidneys).
inclusions: aneurysm (cirsoid)(false)(ruptured)

name: Other peripheral vascular diseases
description: A condition where cold temperatures or intense emotional stress cause the small blood vessels in the fingers and toes to overreact and suddenly spasm shut. The spasm temporarily cuts off blood flow, causing the fingers or toes to turn stark white, then blue, and feel intensely cold or numb. As blood returns, they turn red and throb or tingle.

name: Peripheral vascular disease, unspecified
inclusions: Intermittent claudication | Spasm of artery

name: Arterial embolism and thrombosis
description: A sudden, complete blockage in a body artery caused by a stray blood clot (embolism) or a localized clot that formed directly inside the vessel (thrombosis).
inclusions: infarction: embolic | infarction: thrombotic | occlusion: embolic | occlusion: thrombotic

name: Other disorders of arteries and arterioles
description: A catch-all category for non-plaque artery issues, such as fibromuscular dysplasia or spontaneous arterial tears.

name: Stricture of artery
description: An abnormal, localized narrowing or tightening of an artery that limits downstream blood flow.

name: Diseases of capillaries
description: Disorders affecting the microscopic, single-cell-thick blood vessels where oxygen and nutrients are actually exchanged with body tissues.

name: Disorders of arteries, arterioles and capillaries in diseases classified elsewhere
description: Microscopic or large blood vessel damage caused directly by an outside systemic disease, such as advanced diabetes.

name: Phlebitis and thrombophlebitis
inclusions: endophlebitis | inflammation, vein | periphlebitis | suppurative phlebitis

name: Other venous embolism and thrombosis
description: Blood clots forming inside the veins (the vessels carrying blood back to the heart), most notably including Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) in the legs.

name: Haemorrhoids
description: Swollen, inflamed, and varicosed veins located in the lowest part of the rectum and anus, which can cause pain, itching, or bleeding.

name: Oesophageal varices
description: Abnormally enlarged, swollen veins in the lining of the lower esophagus (the swallowing tube).This is usually caused by severe liver scarring (cirrhosis), which blocks normal blood flow through the liver and forces blood back into these fragile veins. If they rupture, they cause severe, life-threatening internal vomiting of blood.

name: Oesophageal varices without bleeding
description: This code tracks a serious condition where the veins in the walls of the lower esophagus (the swallowing tube connecting the mouth to the stomach) have become abnormally swollen, stretched out, and widened. At the time this code is used, these fragile veins are intact and have not ruptured or bled. This is almost always a secondary complication of severe liver disease (like cirrhosis). Scar tissue in the liver acts like a traffic jam, blocking normal blood flow. To get back to the heart, the blood is forced to take an alternate detour through the tiny, fragile veins of the throat. Because these vessels were never designed to hold that much volume, they swell under the massive pressure (known as portal hypertension).
inclusions: Oesophageal varices NOS

name: Varicose veins of other sites
description: Stretched, twisted, and enlarged veins occurring in areas of the body other than the legs or rectum (such as pelvic or scrotal varicose veins).

name: Other disorders of veins
description: Includes Chronic Venous Insufficiency, where the tiny one-way valves inside the leg veins weaken and fail, allowing blood to pool in the lower legs instead of traveling back up to the heart.

name: Other noninfective disorders of lymphatic vessels and lymph nodes
description: Conditions like lymphedema, where the body's fluid drainage network is blocked, causing chronic, heavy swelling in an arm or leg.

name: Hypotension
description: The clinical term for abnormally low blood pressure (typically a reading below 90/60 mmHg).Unlike high blood pressure, which strains vessels over time, low blood pressure means there isn't enough pushing force to drive oxygen-rich blood up to your brain and vital organs. It can be caused by dehydration, severe infections (sepsis), heart issues, or certain medications.

name: Postprocedural disorders of circulatory system, not elsewhere classified
description: An administrative code used when a patient develops a cardiovascular problem as a direct result of a medical procedure or surgery. This covers issues that happen during or immediately after a surgery, such as a patient's blood pressure bottoming out on the operating table, structural damage to a blood vessel caused by a surgical tool, or a heart rhythm issue triggered by anesthesia.

name: Other disorders of circulatory system in diseases classified elsewhere
description: This is a secondary code used when an underlying illness—like a metabolic disorder or an autoimmune disease—actively damages or degrades the blood vessels and heart.

name: Other disorders of circulatory system in diseases classified elsewhere
description: This code specifically links swollen throat veins (esophageal varices) directly to a primary underlying condition, most commonly cirrhosis or severe liver failure.As scar tissue takes over the liver, it blocks normal blood flow. Blood is forced to take a high-pressure detour through the tiny, fragile veins lining the lower throat. Because these vessels aren't built for that much volume, they swell up like balloons. The "A" in your specific system typically denotes an active or monitored chronic presentation.

name: Other and unspecified disorders of circulatory system
description: A broad, final catch-all billing code. It is used when a patient clearly has a disorder affecting their blood vessels or circulation, but the medical record does not provide enough specific details to assign it to a precise disease.

name: Acute tonsillitis
description: A sudden, rapid-onset infection and inflammation of the tonsils (the two oval-shaped pads of tissue at the back of the throat). Usually caused by a common cold virus or a bacterial infection (like Strep throat), the immune system floods the tonsils with white blood cells to fight the invader. This causes the throat tissue to swell drastically, turn bright red, and often become coated in painful white spots of pus.

name: Acute upper respiratory infections of multiple and unspecified sites
description: The standard medical classification for a severe common cold or general upper airway viral infection that hits the throat, nose, and sinuses all at once.

name: Influenza due to identified seasonal influenza virus
description: Flu symptoms confirmed by a lab to be caused by a specific, known seasonal virus strain (such as standard Influenza A or B), but excluding rare avian or novel pandemic strains. The virus infects the upper and lower respiratory tracts, causing inflammation, cell death in the airway linings, and a massive immune system response. This triggers high fevers, body aches, severe fatigue, dry cough, and a sore throat.

name: Bacterial pneumonia, not elsewhere classified
description: Lung infections caused by other specific bacteria, such as Staphylococcus or H. influenzae.
inclusions: bronchopneumonia due to bacteria other than S. pneumoniae and H. influenzae

name: Pneumonia in diseases classified elsewhere
description: J17 is a unique "manifestation code". It means the patient has pneumonia (a severe lung infection), but it is a direct consequence or symptom of a completely different primary disease that belongs to another area of medicine (like a tropical parasite, fungal infection, or bacterial disease).The primary disease organism travels through the bloodstream or is inhaled, invading the lower lungs. The immune system rushes white blood cells to fight the infection, causing the tiny air sacs (alveoli) inside the lungs to swell and fill up with inflammatory fluid or pus. This physically blocks oxygen from passing into the blood.

name: Pneumonia, organism unspecified
description: The patient definitively has pneumonia visible on a chest X-ray, but the exact bug (bacterial, viral, or fungal) has not been caught in a lab culture yet.

name: Unspecified acute lower respiratory infection
description: A sudden, short-term infection of the deep airways (such as the bronchial tubes or lungs) where the doctor cannot pinpoint the exact location or specific virus/bacteria.The lower respiratory tract becomes inflamed and irritated, causing excess mucus production and airway swelling.
inclusions: Acute (lower) respiratory (tract) infection NOS

name: Chronic diseases of tonsils and adenoids
description: : Long-standing, permanent swelling or repeat infections of the tonsils (throat tissue) or adenoids (tissue high in the back of the nose). Due to chronic inflammation, these tissues grow abnormally large. Instead of protecting the body from germs, they become permanently clogged with bacteria, causing breathing and swallowing pathways to physically narrow.

name: Peritonsillar abscess
description: A critical, severe complication of tonsillitis, commonly known as Quinsy. An infection rips past the tonsil and forms a hidden pocket of active pus deep inside the surrounding throat tissue. The abscess swells rapidly, pushing the tonsil and the uvula (the hanging tissue at the back of the throat) toward the opposite side, which can physically close off the airway.
inclusions: Abscess of tonsil | Peritonsillar cellulitis | Quinsy

name: Diseases of vocal cords and larynx, not elsewhere classified
description: Structural or nerve damage directly affecting the voice box (larynx) and the vocal cords.This covers physical issues like vocal cord nodules/polyps (calluses from screaming or straining the voice), or vocal cord paralysis (where the nerve controlling the vocal cord is severed during surgery or damaged by a virus, leaving it frozen open or closed).

name: Other diseases of upper respiratory tract
description: A general administrative category for upper airway conditions that do not fit neatly into standard infections or allergies.

name: Bronchitis, not specified as acute or chronic
description: General inflammation of the main bronchial breathing tubes inside the lungs, used when the medical record doesn't specify if it is a short-term bug or a lifetime illness. The inner lining of the airways becomes red and irritated, causing the mucus glands to overproduce thick phlegm.
inclusions: Bronchitis: NOS | Bronchitis: catarrhal | Bronchitis: with tracheitis NOS | Tracheobronchitis NOS

name: Emphysema
description: A progressive, destructive lung disease where the lung's air sacs lose their elasticity and structural shape.

name: Emphysema, unspecified
description: Standard, classic emphysema.WStrongly linked to long-term cigarette smoking, the walls of the millions of microscopic air sacs (alveoli) are permanently burned away and destroyed. Instead of thousands of tiny, elastic bubbles that expand and deflate, they rupture into massive, floppy, inelastic holes. Air becomes physically trapped inside these dead spaces, meaning the body cannot push old carbon dioxide out to let fresh oxygen in.
inclusions: Emphysema (lung)(pulmonary): NOS | Emphysema (lung)(pulmonary): bullous | Emphysema (lung)(pulmonary): vesicular | Emphysematous bleb

name: Other chronic obstructive pulmonary disease
description: The overarching parent medical category for COPD—a long-term, progressive umbrella disease that blocks airflow and makes it incredibly difficult to breathe. It typically combines elements of emphysema and chronic bronchitis.
inclusions: chronic: bronchitis: asthmatic (obstructive) | chronic: bronchitis: emphysematous | chronic: bronchitis: with: airways obstruction | chronic: bronchitis: with: emphysema | chronic: obstructive: asthma | chronic: obstructive: bronchitis | chronic: obstructive: tracheobronchitis

name: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease with acute lower respiratory infection
description: A dangerous clinical event where a patient with baseline COPD catches a sudden lung infection (like acute bronchitis or pneumonia).Because the patient’s lungs are already severely compromised, a minor viral or bacterial infection triggers massive inflammation. The airways swell shut further, mucus production explodes, and the patient plunges into acute respiratory distress, often requiring emergency oxygen or hospitalization.

name: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease with acute exacerbation, unspecified
description: This code tracks an acute, sudden COPD flare-up. Unlike J44.0 (which is triggered by a specific, diagnosed infection like pneumonia), an exacerbation means the patient's breathing has suddenly spiraled out of control due to environmental triggers like heavy smog, cold weather, cigarette smoke, or high stress. The baseline inflammation inside the damaged bronchial tubes suddenly explodes. The smooth muscles around the airways spasm and tighten, while the mucus glands go into overdrive, flooding the narrowed pathways with thick phlegm. Air becomes severely trapped inside the lungs, and the patient cannot physically exhale enough carbon dioxide, causing oxygen levels in the blood to drop rapidly. This is a serious event that often requires emergency department treatment.

name: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, unspecified
description: The standard billing code used for a patient with normal, daily, stable COPD. It indicates they have chronic airflow limitation, but are currently at their normal baseline without an active emergency or infection flare-up.
inclusions: Chronic obstructive: airway disease NOS | Chronic obstructive: lung disease NOS

name: Asthma
description: Chronic, highly sensitive airways that suddenly swell shut, fill with mucus, and go into severe muscle spasms when exposed to a trigger (like dust, cold air, or exercise).

name: Predominantly allergic asthma
description: Asthma that is directly triggered by an allergic immune response to environmental particles. When the patient inhales an allergen (like pollen, dust mites, pet dander, or mold), the body mistakenly treats it as a dangerous threat. It floods the lungs with IgE antibodies, which causes the airways to swell, secrete thick mucus, and experience sudden muscle spasms (bronchospasms).
inclusions: Allergic: bronchitis NOS | Allergic: rhinitis with asthma | Atopic asthma | Extrinsic allergic asthma | Hay fever with asthma

name: Asthma, unspecified
description: The standard, general billing code used for a patient diagnosed with chronic asthma when the medical record does not explicitly specify whether it is allergic, non-allergic, or exercise-induced.
inclusions: Asthmatic bronchitis NOS | Late-onset asthma

name: Bronchiectasis
description: A chronic, progressive condition where the large bronchial tubes in the lungs become permanently stretched out, widened, and severely scarred.What happens to the body: Usually caused by severe, repeated childhood lung infections or conditions like Cystic Fibrosis, the airway walls lose their natural muscular support and become floppy. Because the tubes are abnormally wide and limp, they act like traps, allowing heavy pools of thick mucus to settle deep in the lungs. This constant pool of stagnant mucus becomes a breeding ground for chronic bacterial growth.
inclusions: Bronchiolectasis

name: Bronchiectasis
description: A chronic, progressive condition where the large bronchial tubes in the lungs become permanently stretched out, widened, and severely scarred.What happens to the body: Usually caused by severe, repeated childhood lung infections or conditions like Cystic Fibrosis, the airway walls lose their natural muscular support and become floppy. Because the tubes are abnormally wide and limp, they act like traps, allowing heavy pools of thick mucus to settle deep in the lungs. This constant pool of stagnant mucus becomes a breeding ground for chronic bacterial growth.
inclusions: Bronchiolectasis

name: Pneumonitis due to solids and liquids
description: Commonly known as Aspiration Pneumonitis. It occurs when foreign material is accidentally swallowed down the windpipe into the lungs instead of going down the esophagus into the stomach.  Most frequently happening to individuals with swallowing disorders, stroke survivors, or during periods of unconsciousness, highly caustic stomach acid, vomit, or food particles travel into the lungs. The stomach acid chemically burns and dissolves the delicate lung tissues on contact, causing rapid, severe inflammation followed quickly by an aggressive bacterial infection.

name: Respiratory conditions due to other external agents
description: Lung damage or sudden inflammation caused by inhaling non-dust chemicals, environmental gases, fumes, or as a toxic side effect from taking certain medications (like chemotherapy drugs or certain heart pills).

name: Pulmonary oedema
description: An acute, severe medical event where the lungs physically fill up with fluid. It is most commonly caused by advanced congestive heart failure.When the heart muscle weakens, it can no longer pump blood forward efficiently. Blood backs up into the veins of the lungs, causing the blood pressure inside those tiny vessels to spike. The intense pressure forces clear fluid to leak straight through the vessel walls and flood into the air sacs (alveoli). This leaves no room for oxygen exchange, essentially causing the patient to drown internally.
inclusions: Acute oedema of lung | Pulmonary congestion (passive)

name: Other interstitial pulmonary diseases
description: A large family of chronic conditions characterized by progressive, irreversible scarring of the deep framework of tissues (the interstitium) that supports the air sacs. This includes conditions like Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis (IPF). The delicate, stretchy tissue between the air sacs becomes inflamed, thick, and is gradually replaced by stiff, hard scar tissue. The lungs lose their elasticity, making them feel like stiff leather, which severely limits how much air they can hold and makes it incredibly difficult for oxygen to pass into the bloodstream.

name: Pyothorax
description: A severe, advanced complication where a massive, walled-off pocket of active pus collects inside the pleural cavity (the space between the lung and the chest wall).Usually happening when a severe case of bacterial pneumonia is left untreated, the bacteria breach the lung tissue and invade the surrounding chest space. The body tries to trap the infection, creating a thick, painful pocket of infection that presses heavily against the lung, preventing it from expanding.
inclusions: abscess of: pleura | abscess of: thorax | empyema | pyopneumothorax

name: Pleural effusion, not elsewhere classified
description: An abnormal buildup of clear fluid in the space between the lung and the inside of the chest wall.The fluid collects outside the lung tissue. As the volume grows (sometimes reaching several liters), it acts like a heavy, expanding weight that physically crushes the lung, causing it to partially deflate.
inclusions: Pleurisy with effusion

name: Pleural effusion in conditions classified elsewhere
description: A fluid buildup around the lungs that is explicitly caused by an outside, diagnosed disease. The fluid accumulation is triggered directly by systemic conditions such as advanced kidney failure, liver cirrhosis, or the spread of cancer cells to the lining of the lungs.

name: Pneumothorax
description: A collapsed lung. Air escapes out of the lung tissue and leaks into the pleural space (the cavity between the lung and the chest wall). As air builds up outside the lung, it creates intense, expanding physical pressure that squashes the lung tissue, causing a section or the entire lung to deflate like a popped balloon.

name: Other pleural conditions
description: A category for non-infectious, complex fluids filling the chest cavity around the lungs. This includes hemothorax (blood filling the chest cavity after a blood vessel tear) or chylothorax (lymphatic fluid leaking around the lungs).Liquid fills the space surrounding the lung tissue. Because liquid cannot be compressed, it takes up the physical space needed for the lungs to expand, severely restricting a person's ability to pull in air.

name: Postprocedural respiratory disorders, not elsewhere classified
description: An administrative category used when a patient develops a severe lung problem as a direct consequence of a surgery, medical procedure, or mechanical ventilation. This covers a wide range of specific post-surgical issues, including:Ventilator-Induced Lung Injury: Damage to the air sacs caused by the high pressure of a breathing machine.Accidental Puncture: A surgical needle or tool accidentally nicking the lung lining during a biopsy, causing a sudden air leak.Post-Surgical Airway Collapse (Atelectasis): Shallow breathing after receiving heavy anesthesia causing sections of the lower lungs to temporarily deflate.

name: Respiratory failure, not elsewhere classified
description: A critical, life-threatening medical emergency where the lungs can no longer perform their basic job of gas exchange. The respiratory system fails in one of two ways, or both simultaneously:Hypoxemic (Type 1): The lungs cannot transfer enough fresh oxygen into the bloodstream, causing oxygen levels to plummet.Hypercapnic (Type 2): The lungs cannot physically exhale enough waste carbon dioxide, causing the blood to become highly acidic (respiratory acidosis).

name: Other respiratory disorders
description: A final diagnostic family tracking structural defects of the deep lung tissues or airway passages that do not fit into infections or chronic diseases.This includes conditions like tracheomalacia (the cartilage walls of the windpipe become floppy and collapse inward during breathing) or interstitial emphysema (air gets trapped in the structural tissue webs between the air sacs, disrupting normal anatomy).

name: Disorders of tooth development and eruption
description: Structural abnormalities that happen when teeth are forming in children, such as missing teeth (anodontia), extra teeth, or teeth erupting out of order.

name: Dental caries
description: The medical term for tooth decay or cavities. Bacteria in the mouth feed on sugars, producing acid that strips away the hard, outer tooth enamel. Over time, this acid eats deep into the tooth's inner layers.

name: Stomatitis and related lesions
description: Active inflammation of the soft tissues lining the inside of the mouth, most commonly including severe canker sores or mouth ulcers.

name: Oesophagitis
description: Inflammation and swelling of the lining of the esophagus (the swallowing tube). It is frequently caused by chronic acid reflux or food allergies.
inclusions: Abscess of oesophagus | Oesophagitis: NOS | Oesophagitis: chemical | Oesophagitis: peptic

name: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease
description: Chronic acid reflux. The muscular ring (lower esophageal sphincter) at the top of the stomach is weak or fails to close tightly. Corrosive stomach acid and digestive juices splash upward into the unprotected swallowing tube, chemically burning the lining.

name: Other diseases of oesophagus
description: A category for structural defects in the throat tube, such as a narrowing (stricture) from chronic acid scar tissue, or painful tears in the lining.

name: Gastric ulcer
description: The overarching parent medical category for an open sore on the inside of the stomach wall.Where a sudden, rapid-onset stomach ulcer that has breached a blood vessel and is actively bleeding.What happens to the body: Corrosive stomach juices eat deep into an artery on the stomach wall. Blood pours into the digestive tract, which can quickly drop a patient's blood pressure, causing them to go into shock.
inclusions: erosion (acute) of stomach | ulcer (peptic): pylorus | ulcer (peptic): stomach

name: Gastric ulcer
description: A sudden, rapid-onset stomach ulcer that has breached a blood vessel and is actively bleeding.What happens to the body: Corrosive stomach juices eat deep into an artery on the stomach wall. Blood pours into the digestive tract, which can quickly drop a patient's blood pressure, causing them to go into shock.
inclusions: erosion (acute) of stomach | ulcer (peptic): pylorus | ulcer (peptic): stomach

name: Gastric ulcer
description: A long-standing, chronic stomach ulcer that is currently bleeding.
inclusions: erosion (acute) of stomach | ulcer (peptic): pylorus | ulcer (peptic): stomach

name: Gastric ulcer
description: A chronic stomach ulcer that is causing continuous pain but is stable, meaning it is not currently bleeding and has not ruptured.
inclusions: erosion (acute) of stomach | ulcer (peptic): pylorus | ulcer (peptic): stomach

name: Gastric ulcer
description: The standard diagnostic code used when a patient has a typical, non-emergency stomach ulcer without specific structural complications.
inclusions: erosion (acute) of stomach | ulcer (peptic): pylorus | ulcer (peptic): stomach

name: Duodenal ulcer
description: The overarching parent medical category for an open sore in the upper small intestine.
inclusions: erosion (acute) of duodenum | ulcer (peptic): duodenal | ulcer (peptic): postpyloric

name: Duodenal ulcer, acute with hemorrhage
description: A sudden, rapid-onset intestinal sore that is actively bleeding.
inclusions: erosion (acute) of duodenum | ulcer (peptic): duodenal | ulcer (peptic): postpyloric

name: Duodenal ulcer, acute without hemorrhage or perforation
description: A sudden, painful intestinal sore that is causing intense symptoms but has not started bleeding or ruptured yet.
inclusions: erosion (acute) of duodenum | ulcer (peptic): duodenal | ulcer (peptic): postpyloric

name: Duodenal ulcer, chronic or unspecified with hemorrhage
description: A long-standing, recurring intestinal ulcer that has breached a blood vessel and is actively bleeding.
inclusions: erosion (acute) of duodenum | ulcer (peptic): duodenal | ulcer (peptic): postpyloric

name: Duodenal ulcer, acute without hemorrhage or perforation
description: A sudden, painful sore in the first part of the small intestine that is not actively bleeding or ruptured.
inclusions: erosion (acute) of duodenum | ulcer (peptic): duodenal | ulcer (peptic): postpyloric

name: Duodenal ulcer, chronic without hemorrhage or perforation
description: A long-standing, recurring sore in the small intestine that remains stable without bleeding.
inclusions: erosion (acute) of duodenum | ulcer (peptic): duodenal | ulcer (peptic): postpyloric

name: Duodenal ulcer, unspecified as acute or chronic, without hemorrhage or perforation
description: A general diagnostic label for a standard intestinal ulcer with no immediate structural complications.
inclusions: erosion (acute) of duodenum | ulcer (peptic): duodenal | ulcer (peptic): postpyloric

name: Peptic ulcer, site unspecified
description: An open sore somewhere in the digestive tract, used when the medical chart does not clarify if it is in the stomach or the small intestine.
inclusions: gastroduodenal ulcer NOS | peptic ulcer NOS

name: Peptic ulcer, site unspecified
description: A general peptic ulcer that is not bleeding and has not ruptured.
inclusions: gastroduodenal ulcer NOS | peptic ulcer NOS

name: Gastritis and duodenitis
description: Widespread irritation, redness, and inflammation of the stomach lining or the upper small intestine.

name: Functional dyspepsia
description: Chronic, recurring indigestion. The patient experiences bloating, nausea, and upper belly pain without any visible physical sores or ulcers.
inclusions: Indigestion

name: Other diseases of stomach and duodenum
description: A category for miscellaneous structural issues, such as a delayed stomach emptying (gastroparesis).
inclusions: functional disorders of stomach

name: Acute appendicitis
description: Sudden, severe inflammation of the appendix. If left untreated, the pressure can cause it to burst, leaking dangerous bacteria into the abdomen.

name: Other appendicitis
description: Chronic, low-grade appendix irritation or benign growths within the appendix tissue.
inclusions: Appendicitis: chronic | Appendicitis: recurrent

name: Inguinal hernia
description: A groin hernia. Part of the intestine or abdominal fat pushes through a weak spot in the lower abdominal muscle wall.
inclusions: bubonocele | inguinal hernia: NOS | inguinal hernia: direct | inguinal hernia: double | inguinal hernia: indirect | inguinal hernia: oblique | scrotal hernia

name: Umbilical hernia
description: A hernia occurring at the belly button, very common in infants or adults under heavy abdominal strain.
inclusions: paraumbilical hernia

name: Ventral hernia
description: A hernia that breaks through the front wall of the abdominal muscles, often developing at the site of an old surgical scar (incisional hernia).

name: Diaphragmatic hernia
description: An internal hernia where stomach tissue pushes upward through the diaphragm muscle into the chest cavity (often called a hiatal hernia).
inclusions: hiatus hernia (oesophageal)(sliding) | paraoesophageal hernia

name: Crohn disease [regional enteritis]
description: A chronic, autoimmune Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD). The immune system mistakenly attacks the digestive tract, causing deep, patchy ulcers and severe swelling that can span from the mouth to the colon.
inclusions: granulomatous enteritis

name: Ulcerative colitis
description: A sister autoimmune IBD condition to Crohn's. Unlike Crohn's, the inflammation is continuous and strictly confined to the inner lining of the large intestine (colon) and rectum.

name: Other noninfective gastroenteritis and colitis
description: Inflammation of the stomach and intestines caused by food allergies, radiation therapy, or restricted blood flow rather than a standard stomach bug.

name: Vascular disorders of intestine
description: Ischemic Bowel Disease. Restricted blood flow to the intestines caused by narrowed or blocked abdominal arteries.

name: Vascular disorder of intestine, unspecified
description: This code is used when a patient has a circulatory problem affecting their intestines—most commonly known as ischemic bowel disease or mesenteric ischemia—but the medical chart doesn't specify if it is a sudden emergency (acute) or a long-standing issue (chronic). The intestines require a massive, constant supply of oxygen-rich blood via the mesenteric arteries to digest food and keep the bowel tissue alive. In a vascular disorder, these blood vessels become dangerously narrowed by plaque buildup or completely blocked by a blood clot. Because the blood supply is restricted, the intestinal walls become starved of oxygen and nutrients. If left untreated, this lack of blood flow can cause the bowel tissue to rapidly die, rot, and tear open (perforate), leaking dangerous bacteria directly into the abdominal cavity.
inclusions: Ischaemic: colitis NOS | Ischaemic: enteritis NOS | Ischaemic: enterocolitis NOS

name: Paralytic ileus and intestinal obstruction without hernia
description: A bowel blockage. This happens when the intestines are physically blocked by scar tissue (adhesions) or when the bowel muscles temporarily stop working and paralyze (paralytic ileus), causing food and gas to back up dangerously

name: Diverticular disease of intestine
description: The presence of diverticula—small, marble-sized pouches that bulge outward through weak spots in the colon wall.
inclusions: diverticulitis of (small)(large) intestine | diverticulosis of (small)(large) intestine | diverticulum of (small)(large) intestine

name: Other functional intestinal disorders
description: This code acts as a master category for digestive problems where the intestines or colon are structurally normal, but their movement, muscles, or nerves are not functioning correctly. The physical anatomy looks fine on scans, but the bowel struggles to process and move food waste out of the body. The colon relies on synchronized, wave-like muscle contractions (peristalsis) to push waste along. If the nerve signals from the brain are disrupted, or the muscles are too tight or too lazy, waste moves unevenly. This results in severe backups, spasms, or rapid passage of stool.

name: Acute anal fissure
description: A sudden, fresh tear or crack in the lining of the anal canal.This is typically caused by passing a large, hard stool or experiencing severe, prolonged diarrhea. Every time the muscle contracts during a bowel movement, the tear is physically pulled open again, causing sharp, severe pain.

name: Abscess of anal and rectal regions
description: A painful, hidden pocket of active pus that forms deep in the tissues surrounding the anus or rectum, requiring surgical drainage.
inclusions: abscess of anal and rectal regions with or without fistula | cellulitis of anal and rectal regions with or without fistula

name: Other diseases of anus and rectum
description: Conditions like anal fissures (painful tears in the lining) or rectal prolapse.
inclusions: anal canal

name: Other diseases of intestine
description: A catch-all category for miscellaneous bowel issues, such as intestinal polyps or ulcers.

name: Haemorrhoids and perianal venous thrombosis
description: Swollen, inflamed veins located around the anus or lower rectum that can throb, itch, or bleed during bowel movements.
inclusions: piles

name: Peritonitis
description: This is a severe, life-threatening medical emergency. It indicates sudden, widespread inflammation or infection of the peritoneum—the smooth, thin membrane that lines the inside of your abdominal cavity and wraps around all your internal organs.The abdominal cavity is normally a perfectly sterile environment. Peritonitis happens when bacteria or digestive fluids breach this space. This is usually caused by a perforation (a hole tearing open) in another organ—such as a burst appendix (K35), a ruptured stomach ulcer (K25), or a torn colon wall from diverticulitis (K57). When stomach acid, bile, or stool leaks into the sterile cavity, it causes an immediate, massive inflammatory response. The body tries to seal off the infection, but bacteria can rapidly enter the bloodstream, causing septic shock and multi-organ failure.

name: Other disorders of peritoneum
description: This category tracks non-emergency, chronic structural problems affecting the lining of the abdominal cavity. Instead of an active, spreading infection, it primarily deals with structural changes, scar tissue, or abnormal fluid collections. The most common condition billed under this code is abdominal adhesions (K66.0). Adhesions are bands of fibrous scar tissue that form inside the abdomen. They act like internal glue or ropes, causing organs (like the loops of the small intestine) to abnormally stick to each other or to the abdominal wall. These scar tissues usually develop following a previous abdominal surgery, an old bout of appendicitis, or pelvic inflammatory disease. Over time, these tight bands can twist, pull, or kink the intestines, leading to mechanical bowel obstructions

name: Alcoholic liver disease
description: Damage and inflammation to the liver caused by long-term, heavy alcohol use, tracking across stages from fatty liver to alcoholic hepatitis.

name: Alcoholic hepatitis
description: Severe, acute inflammation of the liver caused by heavy alcohol consumption over time. It can happen as a sudden crisis or as a chronic, burning condition. As the liver breaks down alcohol, the chemical process creates highly toxic byproducts. These toxins cause healthy liver cells to swell up and balloon out, triggering a massive inflammatory immune response. If alcohol intake continues, the inflamed cells die off and are replaced by initial webs of scar tissue.

name: Alcoholic cirrhosis of liver
description: Advanced, irreversible scarring of the liver caused by long-term alcohol damage. After years of continuous inflammation, the liver's natural ability to regenerate fails. Healthy liver tissue is permanently replaced by hard, non-functioning scar tissue (cirrhosis) . This thick scar tissue acts like a physical dam, blocking normal blood flow from filtering through the liver. Blood backs up into the veins of the digestive tract and esophagus, creating dangerous high pressure (portal hypertension) and forcing clear fluid to leak out and pool in the belly cavity (ascites).
inclusions: Alcoholic cirrhosis NOS

name: Alcoholic hepatic failure
description: A critical, life-threatening milestone where alcoholic liver disease progresses to complete organ failure.The liver can no longer perform its vital daily functions. It stops manufacturing essential proteins that let your blood clot, fails to process bilirubin (causing severe jaundice), and cannot filter out normal digestive waste products like ammonia. When ammonia builds up in the bloodstream and reaches the brain, it triggers a dangerous state of confusion and altered consciousness known as hepatic encephalopathy.
inclusions: Alcoholic hepatic failure: NOS | Alcoholic hepatic failure: acute | Alcoholic hepatic failure: chronic | Alcoholic hepatic failure: subacute | Alcoholic hepatic failure: with or without hepatic coma

name: Alcoholic liver disease, unspecified
description: A broad administrative code used when a patient has a confirmed, documented liver condition caused by alcohol, but the medical record does not yet specify whether it is at the fatty liver stage, the hepatitis stage, or the cirrhosis stage.

name: Alcoholic liver disease
description: A broad administrative code used when a patient has a confirmed, documented liver condition caused by alcohol, but the medical record does not yet specify whether it is at the fatty liver stage, the hepatitis stage, or the cirrhosis stage. The "D" in your specific database system often denotes a chronic or ongoing presentation.

name: Hepatic failure, not elsewhere classified
description: The overarching master category for liver failure that is not caused by alcohol. This covers organ failure brought on by viral infections (like acute Hepatitis B or C), autoimmune attacks, or severe toxin exposures.Widespread destruction of liver cells completely stops the organ's ability to clear toxins, manage energy, or assist with blood clotting. It is a time-sensitive clinical emergency that requires close ICU monitoring to manage complications like brain swelling and kidney failure.
inclusions: hepatic: coma NOS | hepatic: encephalopathy NOS G94.3 | hepatitis: fulminant NEC, with hepatic failure | hepatitis: malignant NEC, with hepatic failure | liver (cell) necrosis with hepatic failure | yellow liver atrophy or dystrophy

name: Hepatic failure, unspecified
description: The standard clinical code used when a patient has definitive, severe liver failure, but the medical record does not specify whether it was a sudden, rapid-onset emergency (acute) or a slow decline over decades (chronic).

name: Fibrosis and cirrhosis of liver
description: The overarching parent medical category for permanent, irreversible liver scarring where normal, spongy liver tissue is systematically replaced by hard scar tissue .

name: Other and unspecified cirrhosis of liver
description: Advanced liver cirrhosis that is caused by non-alcoholic factors. The "D" in your specific tracking database system denotes an active or monitored chronic presentation. The structural liver failure is identical to alcoholic cirrhosis, but the damage is triggered by alternative health issues. The most common causes under this code are chronic Hepatitis C or Hepatitis B viral infections, or advanced metabolic conditions like Non-Alcoholic Steatohepatitis (NASH).
inclusions: Cirrhosis (of liver): NOS | Cirrhosis (of liver): cryptogenic | Cirrhosis (of liver): macronodular | Cirrhosis (of liver): micronodular | Cirrhosis (of liver): mixed type | Cirrhosis (of liver): portal | Cirrhosis (of liver): postnecrotic

name: Fibrosis and cirrhosis of liver
description: Advanced liver cirrhosis that is caused by non-alcoholic factors. The "D" in your specific tracking database system denotes an active or monitored chronic presentation. The structural liver failure is identical to alcoholic cirrhosis, but the damage is triggered by alternative health issues. The most common causes under this code are chronic Hepatitis C or Hepatitis B viral infections, or advanced metabolic conditions like Non-Alcoholic Steatohepatitis (NASH).

name: Other inflammatory liver diseases
description: A category for non-viral, highly active inflammatory diseases of the liver tissue, such as Autoimmune Hepatitis (where the body's immune system turns and directly attacks its own liver cells) or localized liver abscesses.

name: Other diseases of liver
description: The general parent family tracking chronic or structural anomalies of the liver tissue that aren't classified under infections or cirrhosis.

name: Fatty (change of) liver, not elsewhere classified
description: Commonly known as Hepatic Steatosis or Fatty Liver.Large droplets of metabolic fat abnormally accumulate inside the individual cells of the liver. While early stages cause zero permanent damage, if the fat continues to build up, it triggers a baseline inflammatory response that can slowly progress to liver scarring (cirrhosis) over many years.
inclusions: Nonalcoholic fatty liver disease [NAFLD]

name: Portal hypertension
description: A severe, dangerous circulatory complication directly caused by liver cirrhosis. Because the liver is full of hard scar tissue, blood coming from the stomach and intestines cannot filter through it smoothly. The blood hits a physical wall, causing the blood pressure inside the primary liver artery (the portal vein) to spike drastically. To escape this pressure, the blood forces its way backward into the fragile veins of the throat and stomach, causing them to swell up like balloons (esophageal varices, I85), which can rupture and bleed heavily.

name: Hepatorenal syndrome
description: A severe, critical medical emergency where advanced liver failure suddenly triggers complete kidney failure. In advanced cirrhosis, the extreme changes in abdominal blood pressure trick the body into altering its blood flow patterns. The blood vessels feeding the kidneys suddenly and severely clamp shut, starving the kidneys of blood. The kidneys are structurally healthy, but because they are denied blood, they stop filtering waste entirely.

name: Other specified diseases of liver
description: Tracks less common, specialized liver conditions, such as severe liver congestion caused directly by right-sided heart failure.
inclusions: Acquired intrahepatic vascular shunt | Focal nodular hyperplasia of liver | Hepatoptosis | Simple cyst of liver

name: Cholelithiasis
description: Gallstones.Small, pebble-like stones form inside the gallbladder, typically made of hardened, crystallized cholesterol or bile pigments. They can sit silently for years, but if a stone rolls out and blocks the narrow drainage tube (cystic duct), it traps digestive fluid inside the gallbladder, causing intense muscle spasms.

name: Cholecystitis
description: Acute or chronic inflammation of the gallbladder. This is the direct result of a gallstone (K80) getting physically wedged inside the exit tube. Because the bile cannot drain out, the gallbladder stretches, swells, and the trapped fluid becomes infected with bacteria. If left untreated, the gallbladder wall can lose blood flow and rupture, leaking infection into the abdomen.

name: Other diseases of gallbladder
description: A category for non-inflammatory gallbladder issues, such as a shrunken, scarred gallbladder from old gallstones, or noncancerous polyps on the gallbladder wall.

name: Other diseases of biliary tract
description: Conditions affecting the network of drainage tubes (bile ducts) that carry bile from the liver and gallbladder into the small intestine. This tracks issues like biliary strictures (abnormal narrowing of the tubes) or non-infectious inflammation.

name: Acute pancreatitis
description: A sudden, severe medical emergency involving rapid inflammation of the pancreas. Usually triggered by a gallstone blocking the pancreatic duct or heavy alcohol use, the powerful digestive enzymes manufactured by the pancreas become trapped inside the organ. Instead of digesting food in the intestine, the enzymes activate early and begin physically digesting the pancreas itself.
inclusions: Abscess of pancreas | Necrosis of pancreas: acute | Necrosis of pancreas: infective | Pancreatitis: NOS | Pancreatitis: acute (recurrent) | Pancreatitis: haemorrhagic | Pancreatitis: subacute | Pancreatitis: suppurative

name: Other diseases of pancreas
description: Tracks chronic or long-standing pancreatic issues, most notably Chronic Pancreatitis (where long-term inflammation permanently destroys pancreatic tissue, leading to diabetes and an inability to digest food properly).

name: Postprocedural disorders of digestive system, not elsewhere classified
description: An administrative category used when a patient develops a gastrointestinal complication as a direct result of a surgical procedure (such as a leak after bowel surgery or accidental damage to an organ during an operation).

name: Other diseases of digestive system
description: A broad family primarily used to track gastrointestinal bleeding of unknown origin, such as vomiting blood (hematemesis) or passing bloody stools before a doctor can locate the exact source with a camera.

name: Cutaneous abscess, furuncle and carbuncle
chapter: Diseases of the skin and subcutaneous tissue
description: Deep, painful, localized bacterial skin infections, commonly known as boils or skin abscesses.Bacteria (usually Staph) invade a hair follicle or minor skin break. The infection triggers a massive immune response, creating a walled-off pocket of active pus, dead tissue, and inflammation deep within the skin layers.
inclusions: boil | furunculosis

name: Cellulitis
chapter: Diseases of the skin and subcutaneous tissue
description: A severe, spreading bacterial infection of the deep skin layers and the tissues directly underneath.Unlike an abscess, which is walled off in a pocket, cellulitis spreads rapidly and fluidly across the skin tissues. If left untreated, the bacteria can easily break into the lymphatic system (lymphangitis) or the bloodstream, causing systemic sepsis.
inclusions: acute lymphangitis

name: Pilonidal cyst
chapter: Diseases of the skin and subcutaneous tissue
description: A painful infection or fluid pocket that forms at the very top of the cleft of the buttocks, just above the tailbone. It is usually caused by loose hairs embedding themselves into the skin, creating a chronic foreign-body reaction and recurring abscesses.
inclusions: fistula coccygeal or pilonidal | sinus coccygeal or pilonidal

name: Other local infections of skin and subcutaneous tissue
chapter: Diseases of the skin and subcutaneous tissue
description: A category for alternative localized bacterial skin issues, such as impetigo (highly contagious crusting skin sores common in children) or secondary infections of existing wounds.

name: Other dermatitis
chapter: Diseases of the skin and subcutaneous tissue
description: A broad classification for non-infectious skin inflammation, most commonly tracking conditions like Eczema or severe contact dermatitis (allergic reactions to plants, soaps, or metals).

name: Psoriasis
chapter: Diseases of the skin and subcutaneous tissue
description: A chronic, systemic autoimmune  A chronic, systemic autoimmune skin condition.The immune system mistakenly sends signals that accelerate the growth cycle of skin cells. Instead of taking weeks to shed, new skin cells rise to the surface in a matter of days, piling up rapidly and forming thick, inflamed plaques.skin condition.

name: Skin changes due to chronic exposure to nonionizing radiation
chapter: Diseases of the skin and subcutaneous tissue
description: Permanent skin damage caused entirely by long-term, chronic sun exposure (UV radiation). Permanent skin damage caused entirely by long-term, chronic sun exposure (UV radiation). This includes solar keratosis, advanced sun-aging (wrinkling/leathery skin), and sun spots.This includes solar keratosis, advanced sun-aging (wrinkling/leathery skin), and sun spots.

name: Other disorders of pigmentation
chapter: Diseases of the skin and subcutaneous tissue
description: Permanent skin damage caused entirely by long-term, chronic sun exposure (UV radiation). This includes solar keratosis, advanced sun-aging (wrinkling/leathery skin), and sun spots.

name: Seborrhoeic keratosis
chapter: Diseases of the skin and subcutaneous tissue
description: Very common, completely noncancerous (benign) skin growths that appear in older adults. They look like raised, waxy, or scaly lesions that have a distinct "stuck-on" appearance, varying in color from light tan to dark black.
inclusions: basal cell papilloma | dermatosis papulosa nigra | Leser-TrĂ©lat disease

name: Other epidermal thickening
chapter: Diseases of the skin and subcutaneous tissue
description: A category for conditions where the outermost layer of the skin becomes abnormally thick, hard, and leathery. This includes conditions like acquired keratoderma (severe, painful thickening of the skin on the palms of the hands and soles of the feet).

name: Decubitus ulcer and pressure area
chapter: Diseases of the skin and subcutaneous tissue
description: Commonly known as a bedsor or pressure sore.What happens to the body: Constant, prolonged physical pressure on a specific area of the body (usually over bony points like the heels, ankles, hips, or tailbone) pinches and collapses the tiny blood vessels feeding the skin. Starved of oxygen and blood flow, the skin and underlying tissue die off, breaking open into a deep, raw wound. This is highly common in individuals confined to a bed or wheelchair.
inclusions: Bedsore | Plaster ulcer

name: Atrophic disorders of skin
chapter: Diseases of the skin and subcutaneous tissue
description: Conditions where the skin layers or the fatty tissue underneath abnormally shrink, thin out, and lose their natural elasticity. This includes striae atrophicae (severe stretch marks) or skin thinning caused by long-term use of strong steroid creams.

name: Ulcer of lower limb, not elsewhere classified
chapter: Diseases of the skin and subcutaneous tissue
description: A chronic, open leg or foot ulcer that is not caused by direct pressure or bedsores.These wounds are almost always caused by poor circulation, such as venous insufficiency (blood pooling in the lower legs, causing fluid build-up that breaks through the skin) or peripheral artery disease (starving the foot tissue of healthy blood flow). Because the circulation is so poor, minor scrapes or cuts cannot heal, expanding instead into chronic, painful open sores.

name: Other disorders of skin and subcutaneous tissue, not elsewhere classified
chapter: Diseases of the skin and subcutaneous tissue
description: A catch-all family for miscellaneous skin tissue failures, including pyoderma gangrenosum (a rare, painful condition causing large, rapidly expanding ulcers) or localized skin responses to retained surgical stitches.

name: Pyogenic arthritis
chapter: Diseases of the musculoskeletal system and connective tissue
description: Commonly known as Septic Arthritis. This is a severe, destructive medical emergency involving an active bacterial infection inside a joint.Bacteria (frequently Staphylococcus aureus) enter a joint cavity, either through a direct puncture wound, surgery, or by traveling through the bloodstream from an infection somewhere else in the body. The joint space fills rapidly with active pus and bacteria. The intense inflammation can permanently destroy the delicate joint cartilage within days, leading to irreversible joint failure if not treated immediately.

name: Seropositive rheumatoid arthritis
chapter: Diseases of the musculoskeletal system and connective tissue
description: A chronic, systemic autoimmune disease characterized by painful, destructive joint inflammation.What happens to the body: The body's immune system mistakenly sends white blood cells to attack the synovium—the smooth, fluid-producing membrane that lines and protects the inside of your joints. The membrane swells and thickens into a destructive tissue mass (called a pannus) that systematically eats away at the joint cartilage and bone over time. This form is "seropositive," meaning a blood test actively detects the presence of Rheumatoid Factor (RF) antibodies.

name: Other rheumatoid arthritis
chapter: Diseases of the musculoskeletal system and connective tissue
description: Forms of rheumatoid arthritis that do not fit standard criteria, most notably including Seronegative Rheumatoid Arthritis (where the patient has all the physical joint destruction of the disease, but their blood tests come back completely negative for the standard Rheumatoid Factor antibody).

name: Rheumatoid arthritis, unspecified
chapter: Diseases of the musculoskeletal system and connective tissue
description: The standard, general billing code used for a patient diagnosed with classic chronic rheumatoid arthritis when the medical chart does not specify whether it is seropositive or seronegative, or if it is currently affecting multiple joints at once.

name: Other rheumatoid arthritis
chapter: Diseases of the musculoskeletal system and connective tissue
description: A broad administrative placeholder code. It notes the patient has rheumatoid arthritis, but the medical chart fails to specify which joint or limb is currently inflamed.

name: Rheumatoid arthritis, unspecified, hand
chapter: Diseases of the musculoskeletal system and connective tissue
description: Active rheumatoid arthritis explicitly localized to the joints of the hand (such as the knuckles, fingers, or wrist). The immune system attacks the delicate lining of the small hand joints. As the joints swell, it stretches the surrounding tendons and ligaments, which can eventually cause the fingers to drift sideways toward the pinky finger (ulnar deviation) or lock into rigid shapes.

name: Other rheumatoid aRheumatoid arthritis, unspecified, lower legrthritis
chapter: Diseases of the musculoskeletal system and connective tissue
description: Active rheumatoid arthritis localized to the ankle joint or the small tarsal joints of the foot. It causes chronic swelling, making walking painful and altering foot mechanics over time

name: Rheumatoid arthritis, unspecified, multiple sites
chapter: Diseases of the musculoskeletal system and connective tissue
description: Rheumatoid arthritis that is actively flaring up across several different joints simultaneously (e.g., affecting both hands, the knees, and the ankles all at once).

name: Gout
chapter: Diseases of the musculoskeletal system and connective tissue
description: A form of inflammatory arthritis characterized by sudden, excruciating flare-ups of joint pain.Gout is caused by a metabolic buildup of too much uric acid in the bloodstream. If the body makes too much or the kidneys filter out too little, the excess uric acid crystallizes. These tiny, needle-sharp crystals accumulate directly inside a joint space. The immune system treats these crystals like foreign shards of glass, launching a massive, hyper-aggressive inflammatory response.

name: Other arthritis
chapter: Diseases of the musculoskeletal system and connective tissue
description: A category used for general joint inflammation that does not fit into clean categories like rheumatoid or gout, such as allergic arthritis or temporary joint inflammation following an unrelated viral illness.

name: Polyarthrosis
chapter: Diseases of the musculoskeletal system and connective tissue
description: Osteoarthritis that has set into multiple completely separate joints across the body (such as having wear-and-tear arthritis in both hands, the spine, and a hip simultaneously).
inclusions: arthrosis with mention of more than one site

name: Coxarthrosis [arthrosis of hip]
chapter: Diseases of the musculoskeletal system and connective tissue
description: Degeneration of the ball-and-socket joint of the hip.The slick, protective cartilage covering the head of the femur (thigh bone) and the socket in the pelvis slowly wears away. As the cushion thins, bone begins to scrape directly against bone, causing the body to grow painful, jagged bone spurs (osteophytes) to try and protect itself, which locks up hip mobility.

name: Gonarthrosis [arthrosis of knee]
chapter: Diseases of the musculoskeletal system and connective tissue
description: Wear-and-tear degradation of the knee joint.What happens to the body: The shock-absorbing cartilage menisci inside the knee flatten and fray. Bone rubs on bone, leading to chronic joint fluid pooling ("fluid on the knee").

name: Other arthrosis
chapter: Diseases of the musculoskeletal system and connective tissue
description: A code applied when a patient has wear-and-tear arthritis in an unusual or unspecified location, such as a shoulder, elbow, or wrist joint.

name: Acquired deformities of fingers and toes
chapter: Diseases of the musculoskeletal system and connective tissue
description: Structural alterations in the shapes of the fingers or toes that develop later in life due to underlying severe arthritis. This includes conditions like Bunions (Hallux valgus), Hammertoes, or the structural twisting of finger joints caused by long-term joint erosion.

name: Internal derangement of knee
chapter: Diseases of the musculoskeletal system and connective tissue
description: Structural or mechanical failures inside the knee joint capsule that are usually caused by an old injury or chronic instability.This code tracks internal physical tear zones, such as a torn meniscus (the shock-absorbing cartilage disc) or a chronic tear of the Anterior Cruciate Ligament (ACL). When these tissues fail, loose cartilage fragments can float inside the joint or the knee can suddenly buckle or "lock up" during movement.

name: Other joint disorders, not elsewhere classified
chapter: Diseases of the musculoskeletal system and connective tissue
description: A broad diagnostic family used to bill for generalized joint symptoms. It is most frequently used for joint pain (arthralgia), joint stiffness, or localized swelling when a doctor hasn't labeled it as standard osteoarthritis yet

name: Other necrotizing vasculopathies
chapter: Diseases of the musculoskeletal system and connective tissue
description: A family of rare, serious autoimmune conditions characterized by active inflammation of the blood vessels. The immune system mistakenly attacks the walls of arteries and veins. This causes the blood vessels to swell, narrow, or develop scar tissue, which restricts or completely blocks healthy blood flow to vital organs. Conditions under this code include Giant Cell Arteritis (which hits blood vessels in the temples and can cause sudden blindness if untreated) and Wegener's Granulomatosis.

name: Systemic lupus erythematosus, unspecified
chapter: Diseases of the musculoskeletal system and connective tissue
description: f this chronic disease, but the medical chart at that moment does not single out one specific organ complication (like lupus kidney disease or lupus brain inflammation) as the primary focus of the billing claim. Lupus is a complex, systemic autoimmune disease. For reasons not fully understood, the body's immune system experiences a massive regulatory failure. Instead of creating antibodies to fight off external viruses and bacteria, it manufactures "autoantibodies" that mistake the body's own healthy tissues for foreign invaders. These antibodies launch continuous inflammatory attacks against healthy cells, which can systematically damage joints, skin, blood vessels, and internal organs over time.

name: Other systemic involvement of connective tissue
chapter: Diseases of the musculoskeletal system and connective tissue
