description: A category for alternative multi-organ autoimmune diseases. This includes Sjögren's syndrome (where the immune system destroys the moisture-producing glands, causing severe dry mouth and dry eyes) or Polymyalgia Rheumatica.

name: Polymyalgia rheumatica
chapter: Diseases of the musculoskeletal system and connective tissue

name: Other inflammatory spondylopathies
chapter: Diseases of the musculoskeletal system and connective tissue
description: Active inflammation of the spine. A primary condition here is Ankylosing Spondylitis.The immune system causes chronic inflammation in the joints and ligaments of the spinal column. Over time, the body tries to heal this chronic inflammation by growing new bone. This causes the individual vertebrae of the spine to physically fuse together into a rigid, solid block of bone (often called a "bamboo spine"), destroying all flexibility.

name: Spondylosis
chapter: Diseases of the musculoskeletal system and connective tissue
description: Age-related wear-and-tear osteoarthritis of the spine. The slick cartilage cushions between the spinal joints wear thin, and the bones scrape together. To protect itself, the body grows jagged bone spurs (osteophytes) around the edges of the vertebrae. These spurs can physically press outward into the spinal canal or exit holes, pinching passing nerves.
inclusions: arthrosis or osteoarthritis of spine | degeneration of facet joints

name: Other spondylopathies
chapter: Diseases of the musculoskeletal system and connective tissue
description: A category for structural mechanical failures of the spine, such as Spinal Stenosis (an abnormal narrowing of the spinal cord tunnel that crushes the central nervous system cables) or spinal fatigue fractures.

name: Other dorsopathies, not elsewhere classified
chapter: Diseases of the musculoskeletal system and connective tissue
description: Structural issues of the neck and back that aren't caused by arthritis, such as Cervicobrachial syndrome (where neck structural issues cause shooting pain and numbness to travel down the arm).

name: Dorsalgia
chapter: Diseases of the musculoskeletal system and connective tissue
description: The official clinical term for general back pain. This is one of the most common billing codes in healthcare, used for generic lower back pain (lumbago), neck pain, or thoracic spine pain before an MRI can pinpoint a herniated disc or specific arthritic cause

name: Other disorders of muscle
chapter: Diseases of the musculoskeletal system and connective tissue
description: Conditions affecting the actual skeletal muscle fibers. This tracks issues like muscle atrophy (wasting away of muscle tissue from disuse or nerve damage), chronic muscle spasms, or muscle weakness.

name: Other soft tissue disorders, not elsewhere classified
chapter: Diseases of the musculoskeletal system and connective tissue
description: A broad category used to track deep tissue pain that doesn't originate in the bones or joints. This code is frequently used to document Fibromyalgia (a chronic condition causing widespread muscle pain, tenderness, and fatigue throughout the entire body) or localized nerve pain (neuralgia).

name: Osteoporosis with pathological fracture
chapter: Diseases of the musculoskeletal system and connective tissue
description: A serious skeletal condition where a patient's bones are severely weakened by osteoporosis, causing a bone to break during a normal, everyday activity that wouldn't normally cause an injury. Osteoporosis silently thins out the internal scaffolding of the bones, making them brittle like dry twigs. A "pathological fracture" means a bone breaks from minor stress—such as coughing, sneezing, or simply stepping off a curb—rather than from a major fall or accident. It most commonly strikes the vertebrae in the spine, hips, or wrists.
inclusions: osteoporotic vertebral collapse and wedging

name: Osteoporosis without pathological fracture
chapter: Diseases of the musculoskeletal system and connective tissue
description: The standard billing code for baseline osteoporosis. The patient has documented low bone mineral density, placing them at high risk for breaks, but they have not suffered a fracture. It is often a asymptomatic condition monitored via bone density scans (DEXA).

name: Disorders of continuity of bone
chapter: Diseases of the musculoskeletal system and connective tissue
description: A category for complications in how a bone heals after a break. This tracks issues like a nonunion (the broken bone pieces fail to grow back together entirely) or a malunion (the bone heals but is crooked, rotated, or out of normal alignment).

name: Osteomyelitis
chapter: Diseases of the musculoskeletal system and connective tissue
description: A severe, painful bacterial infection deep inside a bone. Bacteria (frequently Staphylococcus) enter the bone tissue through a deep open wound, a surgical procedure, or by traveling through the blood from an infection elsewhere. The infection builds up intense pressure inside the rigid bone cavity, cutting off its own blood supply. If left untreated, sections of the bone can die (necrotize), requiring surgical removal.

name: Osteopathies in diseases classified elsewhere
chapter: Diseases of the musculoskeletal system and connective tissue
description: Bone degradation, death, or structural anomalies caused directly by a separate primary disease, such as bone death caused by sickle cell anemia or advanced tuberculosis.

name: Intraoperative and postprocedural complications and disorders of musculoskeletal system, not elsewhere classified
chapter: Diseases of the musculoskeletal system and connective tissue
description: An administrative category used when a patient develops a bone or joint complication as a direct result of surgery or a medical procedure (such as a bone splitting while a surgeon is implanting an artificial joint).

name: Chronic nephritic syndrome
description: Long-standing, slowly progressing inflammation of the kidney's filtering units (the glomeruli). Over decades, this chronic inflammation leads to irreversible scarring and kidney damage.
inclusions: chronic: glomerular disease | chronic: glomerulonephritis | chronic: nephritis

name: Glomerular disorders in diseases classified elsewhere
description: Severe damage and inflammation to the kidney's filters caused entirely by a separate systemic disease. The most frequent cause billed under this code is Lupus Nephritis (where Lupus, M32.9, directly attacks the kidney filters).
inclusions: nephropathy in diseases classified elsewhere

name: Obstructive and reflux uropathy
description: A structural blockage or reverse flow in the urinary tract that stops urine from draining properly out of the body. Usually caused by a large kidney stone, a tumor, or a narrowed tube (stricture), urine gets trapped and backs up into the kidney. This causes the kidney to stretch out and swell under the fluid pressure (a condition called hydronephrosis), which can permanently destroy kidney tissue within weeks if the blockage isn't cleared.

name: Acute renal failure
description: A sudden, rapid-onset medical emergency where the kidneys abruptly stop working, over a matter of hours or days. It can be caused by severe dehydration, toxic drug exposures, or heavy blood loss. It is often reversible if the root cause is treated quickly.
inclusions: acute renal impairment

name: Chronic kidney disease
description: The overarching parent medical category for permanent, long-term, progressive kidney damage where the organs slowly lose their filtering capacity over years, most commonly caused by long-standing high blood pressure or diabetes.
inclusions: chronic renal failure

name: Chronic kidney disease, stage 3
description: Moderate kidney damage. The kidney's filtering capacity (eGFR) has dropped significantly, sitting between 30% and 59%. At this stage, waste products begin to build up in the blood, and doctors focus heavily on protecting remaining function.

name: Chronic kidney disease, stage 4
description: Severe kidney damage. The organ's filtering capacity has plummeted dangerously low, sitting between 15% and 29%. This is the final stage before full kidney failure, and patients are actively prepared for dialysis or a transplant evaluation.

name: Chronic kidney disease, unspecified
description: The baseline code for permanent, progressive kidney damage where the medical chart confirms chronic kidney disease but lacks structural sub-details or an exact calculated filtering stage (stages 1 to 5) at that moment.
inclusions: Chronic renal impairment | Chronic uraemia NOS | Diffuse sclerosing glomerulonephritis NOS

name: Unspecified kidney failure
description: An emergency placeholder code used when a patient's kidneys have shut down completely, but the diagnostic testing hasn't yet clarified if it is a sudden crisis (acute) or long-term damage (chronic).
inclusions: Renal insufficiency NOS | Uraemia NOS

name: Unspecified kidney failure
description: An emergency placeholder code used when a patient's kidneys have shut down completely, but the diagnostic testing hasn't yet clarified if it is a sudden crisis (acute) or long-term damage (chronic).
inclusions: Renal insufficiency NOS | Uraemia NOS

name: Calculus of lower urinary tract
description: Bladder stones or stones trapped in the lower urinary tubes.Minerals in the urine crystallize and form hard, pebble-like stones inside the bladder. They can roll around and block the exit hole where urine leaves the body, trapping fluid and causing severe tissue irritation.
inclusions: with cystitis and urethritis

name: Unspecified renal colic
description: The official medical term for excruciating, sudden pain caused by a kidney stone passing through the narrow tubes. A jagged stone breaks loose from the kidney and scratches its way down the microscopic tube (ureter) leading to the bladder. The tube goes into violent muscle spasms to try and push the stone out, completely blocking urine flow from that kidney.

name: Disorders resulting from impaired renal tubular function
description: Conditions where the kidney's filtering units are intact, but the microscopic reabsorption tubes fail to manage electrolytes. This tracks issues like renal tubular acidosis, where the kidneys fail to remove acid from the blood, making the body highly acidic.

name: Other disorders of kidney and ureter, not elsewhere classified
description: Tracks structural defects outside of infections or stones, such as a renal infarction (a blood clot cutting off blood flow to the kidney tissue) or a twisted drainage tube.

name: Cystitis
description: Bladder inflammation or infection. Bacteria enter the urethra and multiply rapidly inside the bladder, causing the inner lining to turn bright red, swollen, and raw. It can also be caused by radiation or chemical irritants.

name: Neuromuscular dysfunction of bladder, not elsewhere classified
description: Commonly known as a Neurogenic Bladder.The nerve pathways between the brain, spinal cord, and bladder muscles are damaged (often by diabetes, a stroke, or a spinal injury). The bladder either fails to squeeze waste out completely (urinary retention) or squeezes uncontrollably out of sync (neurogenic urgency).

name: Other disorders of bladder
description: Structural failures of the bladder walls, such as a bladder diverticulum (an abnormal pouch bulging outward through the bladder muscle wall) or a scarred, shrunken bladder lining.

name: Urethral stricture
description: An abnormal narrowing of the urethra (the final tube that carries urine out of the body).Usually caused by old scar tissue from an infection or a medical procedure, the tube tightens and narrows. This acts like a kinked hose, forcing the bladder to generate dangerous pressures to squeeze out urine.

name: Other disorders of urinary system
description: A broad administrative family used to group common general urinary symptoms. It is heavily utilized for a Urinary Tract Infection (UTI), site unspecified or chronic Urinary Incontinence (accidental leaking of urine).

name: Hyperplasia of prostate
description: An enlarged prostate, occurring exclusively in men. The prostate gland sits directly underneath the bladder, completely wrapping around the exit tube (urethra). As men age, the gland naturally grows larger. The expanding tissue pinches down on the urethra like a clamp, making it incredibly difficult for the bladder to empty.
inclusions: Adenofibromatous hypertrophy of prostate | Enlargement (benign) of prostate | Hypertrophy (benign) of prostate | Median bar (prostate) | Prostatic obstruction NOS

name: Orchitis and epididymitis
description: Active inflammation or infection of the testicle (orchitis) and the epididymis (the coiled tube at the back of the testicle that stores sperm).Usually triggered by a bacterial infection or a sexually transmitted infection (STI), the tissues swell rapidly under intense pressure.

name: Redundant prepuce, phimosis and paraphimosis
description: Structural disorders affecting the foreskin. This covers phimosis (where the foreskin is too tight to be pulled back over the head of the penis) and paraphimosis (a medical emergency where the foreskin gets trapped behind the head and acts like a tight rubber band, cutting off blood flow to the tip).
inclusions: Adherent prepuce | Tight foreskin

name: Other disorders of penis
description: Covers localized, non-infectious mechanical anomalies such as balanitis (inflammation of the head of the penis) or structural curvatures (Peyronie's disease).

name: Inflammatory disorders of male genital organs, not elsewhere classified
description: A category for deeper, complex pelvic infections in men, such as a localized abscess in the seminal vesicles or scrotum.

name: Benign mammary dysplasia
description: Commonly known as fibrocystic breast disease. Normal hormonal changes during menstrual cycles cause the breast tissue to form benign, fluid-filled cysts or lumpy, fibrous scar-like tissue.
inclusions: fibrocystic mastopathy

name: Inflammatory disorders of breast
description: Mastitis or a localized breast abscess. Bacteria enter the breast tissue via a cracked nipple (highly common during breastfeeding). The tissue plugs up, swells, and develops a painful infection.
inclusions: Abscess (acute)(chronic)(nonpuerperal) of: areola | Abscess (acute)(chronic)(nonpuerperal) of: breast | Carbuncle of breast | Mastitis (acute)(subacute)(nonpuerperal): NOS | Mastitis (acute)(subacute)(nonpuerperal): infective

name: Unspecified lump in breast
description: A high-frequency diagnostic baseline code used when a physician or patient physically feels an unidentified lump in the breast tissue, acting as a placeholder before an ultrasound, mammogram, or biopsy can confirm if it is a benign cyst or a tumor.
inclusions: Nodule(s) NOS in breast

name: Other disorders of breast
description: Non-cancerous anomalies, such as localized breast pain (mastalgia), nipple discharge, or cysts that do not fit into the fibrocystic category.

name: Inflammatory disease of cervix uteri
description: Cervicitis—inflammation or infection of the cervix (the neck of the womb), often caused by STIs like chlamydia or gonorrhea.
inclusions: Cervicitis with or without erosion or ectropion | Endocervicitis with or without erosion or ectropion | Exocervicitis with or without erosion or ectropion

name: Other female pelvic inflammatory diseases
description: Deep pelvic infections, including Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID) that has spread to the lining of the uterus or surrounding pelvic tissues, risking long-term internal scarring if untreated.

name: Endometriosis
description: A painful, chronic gynecological condition.Tissue that behaves exactly like the inner lining of the womb (the endometrium) abnormally grows outside the uterus—attaching to the ovaries, fallopian tubes, or the outer wall of the intestines. Every month, this displaced tissue responds to hormones by thickening and bleeding, but because the blood has nowhere to escape, it causes severe inflammation, internal bleeding, and dense webs of painful scar tissue (adhesions).

name: Female genital prolapse
description: Structural sagging of the pelvic organs.The pelvic floor muscles and ligaments stretch, weaken, and fail to provide support. This causes the uterus (uterine prolapse), bladder (cystocele), or rectum to physically drop down and bulge into the vaginal canal.

name: Noninflammatory disorders of ovary, fallopian tube and broad ligament
description: Structural anomalies, most frequently utilized to track standard ovarian cysts (fluid-filled sacs that grow on the ovaries) or fallopian tube narrowing without an active bacterial infection.

name: Polyp of female genital tract
description: Noncancerous (benign) finger-like growths that develop along the lining of the female reproductive tract. This most commonly tracks cervical polyps or endometrial polyps growing inside the womb, which can cause irregular spotting.

name: Other noninflammatory disorders of uterus, except cervix
description: Structural uterine conditions, including endometrial hyperplasia (an abnormal thickening of the womb lining, often caused by an excess of estrogen) or a shifted uterus.

name: Dysplasia of cervix uteri
description: Cervical Dysplasia. Abnormal, precancerous cells develop on the surface lining of the cervix, typically triggered by chronic exposure to certain strains of the Human Papillomavirus (HPV). It causes zero physical symptoms and is picked up during routine Pap smear screenings. It is categorized by severity levels (CIN 1, 2, or 3) to guide preventative care.

name: Other noninflammatory disorders of cervix uteri
description: Structural failures of the cervix, such as an incompetent cervix (where the cervix weakens and opens too early during pregnancy) or deep scarring from a past medical procedure.

name: Other noninflammatory disorders of vagina
description: Non-infectious vaginal wall anomalies, such as localized cysts or structural narrowing (strictures).

name: Other noninflammatory disorders of vulva and perineum
description: Conditions affecting the external female genitalia, including chronic skin thinning (lichen sclerosus) or cysts.

name: Excessive, frequent and irregular menstruation
description: A broad diagnostic family for abnormal uterine bleeding, including menorrhagia (abnormally heavy or prolonged periods that can lead to anemia) or periods that occur too frequently.

name: Other abnormal uterine and vaginal bleeding
description: Unexplained spotting or bleeding between periods, or bleeding that occurs after sexual intercourse, before a definitive underlying cause (like a polyp or fibroid) can be diagnosed.

name: Pain and other conditions associated with female genital organs and menstrual cycle
description: Tracks severe menstrual-associated symptoms, heavily utilized for dysmenorrhea (debilitatingly painful period cramps) or severe Mittelschmerz (sharp pain felt on one side during ovulation).

name: Menopausal and other perimenopausal disorders
description: Clinically monitors the physical transitions of menopause or perimenopause. It covers the natural drop in hormone levels and tracks symptoms like severe hot flashes, night sweats, sleep disruptions, and premature menopause.

name: Female infertility
description: The clinical classification utilized when a woman is unable to conceive after a year or more of regular, unprotected intercourse, tracking underlying physiological blocks like fallopian tube obstructions or ovulatory failure.
inclusions: inability to achieve a pregnancy | sterility, female NOS

name: Postprocedural disorders of genitourinary system, not elsewhere classified
description: An administrative category used when a patient develops a kidney, bladder, or reproductive tract complication as a direct consequence of a medical procedure or surgery (such as an accidental injury to the bladder during a hysterectomy or a pelvic infection following a procedure)

name: Congenital malformations of cardiac septa
chapter: Congenital malformations, deformations and chromosomal abnormalities
description: A congenital heart defect where a baby is born with a structural hole in the internal wall (septum) that separates the chambers of the heart. This master code tracks conditions like an Atrial Septal Defect (ASD) or a Ventricular Septal Defect (VSD). Because there is a hole in the dividing wall, oxygen-rich blood mixes abnormally with oxygen-poor blood. This forces the right side of the heart to pump extra volume under high pressure, which can stretch out the heart muscle and overload the blood vessels in the lungs over time.

name: Presence of other cardiac and vascular implants and grafts
chapter: Factors influencing health status and contact with health services
description: An administrative "status code" used to document that a patient has a historically implanted medical device or a surgical bypass graft currently inside their cardiovascular system. It notes the ongoing presence of items like a custom vascular stent, a mechanical heart valve, or a past coronary bypass line during routine medical encounters.
inclusions: Presence of intravascular prosthesis NEC | Status following peripheral angioplasty NOS
